title: University of Michigan

infobox.value: * [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [ORAU](/wiki/Oak_Ridge_Associated_Universities "Oak Ridge Associated Universities") * [URA](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") * [URC](/wiki/University_Research_Corridor "University Research Corridor") * [sea-grant](/wiki/National_Sea_Grant_College_Program "National Sea Grant College Program") * [space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")

[Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan") , United States  [42°16′37″N 83°44′17″W﻿ / ﻿42.27694°N 83.73806°W﻿ / 42.27694; -83.73806](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=University_of_Michigan&params=42_16_37_N_83_44_17_W_)
* *[The Michigan Daily](/wiki/The_Michigan_Daily "The Michigan Daily")* * *[The Michigan Review](/wiki/The_Michigan_Review "The Michigan Review")*

* [NCAA Division I FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS "NCAA Division I FBS") – [Big Ten](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") * [CWPA](/wiki/Collegiate_Water_Polo_Association "Collegiate Water Polo Association")

(**UMich**, **U-M**, or **Michigan**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), United States. It was founded in 1817 and is the oldest institution of higher education in the state. It is also one of the earliest American research universities and is a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").

The **University of Michigan** (**UMich**, **U-M**, or **Michigan**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan"), United States. It was founded in 1817 and is the oldest institution of higher education in the state. It is also one of the earliest American research universities and is a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").

The university has the largest student population in Michigan, enrolling more than 53,000 students, including more than 35,000 undergraduates and 18,000 postgraduates. It is classified as an "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity" by the [Carnegie Classification](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilman2017102-9) It consists of 19 schools and colleges, offering more than 280 degree programs.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDesprezPehlkeZichi2025-10) The university is [accredited](/wiki/Higher_education_accreditation_in_the_United_States "Higher education accreditation in the United States") by the [Higher Learning Commission](/wiki/Higher_Learning_Commission "Higher Learning Commission"). In 2021, it ranked third among American universities in [research expenditures](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_research_and_development_spending "List of countries by research and development spending") according to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation").

The campus spans 3,177 acres (12.86 km2). It encompasses [Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium"), which is the [largest stadium in the United States](/wiki/List_of_U.S._stadiums_by_capacity "List of U.S. stadiums by capacity"), as well as the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"), and ranks [third globally](/wiki/List_of_stadiums_by_capacity "List of stadiums by capacity"). The University of Michigan's athletic teams, including 13 men's teams and 14 women's teams competing in intercollegiate sports, are collectively known as the [Wolverines](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines"). They compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") ([FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision")) as a member of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). Between 1900 and 2022, athletes from the university earned a total of 185 medals at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals"), including 86 gold.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Michigan](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Michigan "History of the University of Michigan")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The proposal for establishing an [institution of higher education](/wiki/Institution_of_higher_education "Institution of higher education") in Michigan dates back to 1703, during the colonial period of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19066-11)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon1856112-12)[[a]](#cite_note-14) Just two years after founding [Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit](/wiki/Fort_Pontchartrain_du_D%C3%A9troit "Fort Pontchartrain du Détroit") on the strait between Lakes [Saint Clair](/wiki/Lake_Saint_Clair "Lake Saint Clair") and [Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") in 1701, the French explorer and later colonial governor of [Louisiana](/wiki/La_Louisiane "La Louisiane"), [Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac](/wiki/Antoine_de_la_Mothe_Cadillac "Antoine de la Mothe Cadillac"), wrote to King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV")'s minister, [Louis Phélypeaux de Pontchartrain](/wiki/Louis_Ph%C3%A9lypeaux_de_Pontchartrain "Louis Phélypeaux de Pontchartrain"), from the settlement under the date of August 31, 1703.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon1856112-12) He urged the establishment of a seminary in the newly formed parish of [Sainte-Anne-de-Détroit](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sainte_Anne_de_D%C3%A9troit "Basilica of Sainte Anne de Détroit") for the education of both Indigenous and French children in piety and the French language.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon1856112-12)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19066-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss189879-15) It remains uncertain whether a seminary was ever established;[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889720-16) the surviving *registres* make no mention of a seminary being created from the proposal.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFullerCatlin1928119–120-17) [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") superiors in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") probably opposed the idea of creating a seminary in the *[Pays d'en Haut](/wiki/Pays_d%27en_Haut "Pays d'en Haut")*, due to disputes with Cadillac and fears of rivalry with their own institutions.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon1856137-18) Parish records from 1755 indicate, nevertheless, that Jean Baptiste Rocoux was "Director of Christian Schools,"[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889720-16) a title likely influenced by [Jean-Baptiste de La Salle](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_de_La_Salle "Jean-Baptiste de La Salle").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19066-11) He kept a school either at his residence on St. Jacques Street or in a building under the patronage of Ste. Anne's Church.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFullerCatlin1928120-19) Mentions are made of a seminary of unknown founding at the fort that trained young men for the ministry,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922-20)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJudson1926268–288-21) but it was destroyed in the [Great Fire of 1805](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_1805 "Great Fire of 1805").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922-20) Additionally, there was a French [subscription school](/wiki/Subscription_school "Subscription school") that stood near the fort in 1775, and an old account book preserved from 1780 contains tuition charges dated as early as 1760.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19066-11)

The colony was surrendered to the British monarchy in 1762 following the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"). The local [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") maintained the Christian schools, which became bilingual;[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMantz197618-22)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19067-23) however, the British, who viewed the colony merely as a trading post, did practically nothing for education, leading to stagnation during their rule from 1763 to 1796.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19067-23)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922700–721-24)

When the colony came under American control following the enforcement of the [1783 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") in 1796, the territorial judges were supposed to be called in to formally define the rights and legal status of the Christian schools under the new constitution. Thereafter, the interpretation of the American right to education in Michigan, grounded in Section 1, Article XI of the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance"), was laid out.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShearman18521-25) Since 1806, parish-minister [Gabriel Richard](/wiki/Gabriel_Richard "Gabriel Richard"), who presided over the Christian schools and helped plan a new layout for Detroit after the [fire in 1805](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_1805 "Great Fire of 1805"),[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195921–22-26) petitioned for land to found an "institution for higher learning" on several occasions[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922723–726-27)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19067-23) and suggested that a lottery might be used to support the academies he headed.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaisnée1952269–270-28)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunbarMay1995188-29) Subsequently, in 1817, during the postwar period following the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the [Territorial government](/wiki/Territory_of_Michigan "Territory of Michigan"), at the instigation of Fr. Richard[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194214–15-30)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19067-23)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922723-31) and Judge [Augustus B. Woodward](/wiki/Augustus_B._Woodward "Augustus B. Woodward")[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194214–15-30)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw19208-32)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922723-31) and with the support of U.S. President [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"),[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194214–15-30) passed "an Act to establish the **Catholepistemiad**, or **University of Michigania**" within the [Territory of Michigan](/wiki/Territory_of_Michigan "Territory of Michigan").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShearman18524–5-33) Enacted on August 26, 1817,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889728-34)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBersey189026-35) the Act effectively consolidated the schools into one institution. Rev. [John Monteith](/wiki/John_Monteith_(minister) "John Monteith (minister)"), a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") minister, was named as its first president[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922–24-36) and Fr. Richard as vice president.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195921–22-26) Its *didactors* had authority not only over the university itself but also over education in the territory in general.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922-20)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19068–10-37) The legislative act was signed into law by Acting Governor and Secretary [William Woodbridge](/wiki/William_Woodbridge "William Woodbridge"), Chief Judge Augustus B. Woodward, and Judge [John Griffin](/wiki/John_Griffin_(judge) "John Griffin (judge)").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLaughlin189129–31-38)

### The Catholepistemiad, 1817–1821

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Catholepistemiad, 1817–1821")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/STEANNESCHURCH1818.jpg/250px-STEANNESCHURCH1818.jpg)](/wiki/File:STEANNESCHURCH1818.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Catholepistemiad_Detroit_Sainte-Anne_1818.png/250px-Catholepistemiad_Detroit_Sainte-Anne_1818.png)](/wiki/File:Catholepistemiad_Detroit_Sainte-Anne_1818.png)

From top to bottom: Ste. Anne's "Stone Church", built in 1818; The Catholepistemiad's University Building (center) on a 1818 map of Detroit. The building fronts on Bates Street directly across from Ste. Anne's "Stone Church" (middle of the lower right).

The university was modeled after an institution established in France a decade earlier, known as the *[Université imperial](/wiki/University_of_France "University of France")*, under Emperor [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190610-39)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw19207–22-40) The term "Catholepistemiad," a neologism derived from a blend of Greek and Latin roots, can be loosely translated as "School of Universal Knowledge".[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunbarMay1995189-41) This entity included an array of schools and libraries under a single administration,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale19068–9-42) with the authority to establish additional schools across the territory.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190610-39)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw19207–22-40) It was not until the legislative council passed the territory's first public school law on April 12, 1827, which made basic education a municipal duty, that the corporation focused solely on higher education.

The university may have initially been funded through private donations and federal land grants.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastillaHodgeson20176–23-43) Shortly after its founding in 1817, it received a $250 subscription from the [Freemason Zion Lodge of Detroit](/wiki/Zion_Lodge_No._1_F%26AM "Zion Lodge No. 1 F&AM").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastillaHodgeson20176–23-43) Of the total amount subscribed to establish the university, two-thirds came from the Zion Lodge and its members.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastillaHodgeson20176–23-43)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190612-44) Early benefactors included [Joseph Campau](/wiki/Joseph_Campau "Joseph Campau") (1769–1863) and his nephew [John R. Williams](/wiki/John_R._Williams "John R. Williams") (1782–1854). The first endowment may have been a land grant from the U.S. federal government on May 20, 1826, as part of "an Act concerning a seminary of learning in the territory of Michigan," based on the [Treaty at the Foot of the Rapids](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fort_Meigs "Treaty of Fort Meigs") and the [Treaty at Detroit](/wiki/Treaty_of_Detroit "Treaty of Detroit").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastillaHodgeson20176–23-43)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922757-45)

The cornerstone of the University Building, located on Bates Street directly across from the 1818 "Stone Church" of [Ste. Anne Parish](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sainte_Anne_de_D%C3%A9troit "Basilica of Sainte Anne de Détroit") in Detroit,[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmerHuntJune1906674–709-46)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195923-47) was laid on September 24, 1817.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190612-44) By the following year, a [Lancasterian school](/wiki/Lancasterian_school "Lancasterian school"), taught by [Lemuel Shattuck](/wiki/Lemuel_Shattuck "Lemuel Shattuck"),[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922-20) and a classical academy, taught by Hugh M. Dickey,[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195923-47) were in operation.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889728–732-48)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190612-44) The [city library of Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Public_Library "Detroit Public Library"), incorporated on August 26, 1817, was initially located in the University Building, with university teachers serving as librarians.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889710-49) Over the years, the University Building was occupied by various teachers,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889715–727-50) including D. B. Crane, who taught Latin and Greek and established a chemistry laboratory in the building.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmerHuntJune1906674–709-46) The University Building later served as the Detroit Branch School after the university moved to Ann Arbor.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889728–732-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/First_Annual_Report_University_of_Michigania.JPG/250px-First_Annual_Report_University_of_Michigania.JPG)](/wiki/File:First_Annual_Report_University_of_Michigania.JPG)

First Annual Report of the *University of Michigania*, authored on November 16, 1818

In 1821, by a new enactment, the university itself was created as a "body politic and corporate",[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw1942117-51) maintaining its corporate status through various modifications to its charter.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190611-52) The new act placed the corporation under the control of a board of trustees.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190612–13-53) Rev. Monteith joined the board shortly but left the university for New York a year later, despite being offered the chairmanship.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoorGriffin195922–24-36) The trustees continued to manage the schools and classical academy, but established no new schools.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190614-54) By 1827, the first public school law mandated that local governments organize their own school districts within each township.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922730–731-55) Thereafter, the university leased the Detroit schoolhouse to private teachers,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190614-54) and the first school in Detroit under this law opened about the first June by an order of the trustees.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922730–731-55) However, the school soon closed following the passing of its teacher.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurtonStockingMiller1922730–731-55) The following year, the [Bridge School](/wiki/Bridge_School_(Michigan) "Bridge School (Michigan)") in [Raisinville Township](/wiki/Raisinville_Township "Raisinville Township") was established under local governance.

### Early years in Ann Arbor, 1837–1851

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early years in Ann Arbor, 1837–1851")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Library_and_Chapel%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_Ann_Arbor_%28front_elevation%29_MET_DT369502.jpg/250px-Library_and_Chapel%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_Ann_Arbor_%28front_elevation%29_MET_DT369502.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_and_Chapel,_University_of_Michigan,_Ann_Arbor_(front_elevation)_MET_DT369502.jpg)

[Alexander J. Davis](/wiki/Alexander_J._Davis "Alexander J. Davis")'s original designs for the university featured the Gothic Revival style. He is generally credited with coining the term "[Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic")"

Following Michigan's admission to the Union in 1837, an organic act was passed on March 18, 1837, to reorganize the university under a twelve-member board of regents.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194232-56) The regents met in [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor "Ann Arbor") and accepted the town's proposal for the university to relocate to Judge [Henry Rumsey](/wiki/Henry_Rumsey "Henry Rumsey")'s farmland.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194232-56)

The approved campus plans for the university were drawn up by the architect [Alexander Davis](/wiki/Alexander_Jackson_Davis "Alexander Jackson Davis").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruettner20031,_9-57)[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMayer2015-58) Davis designed an elaborate [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") main building with a large lawn in front, wide avenues, and botanical gardens, all arranged to evoke the French *[château](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau "Château")* aesthetic.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruettner20031,_9-57) He also provided possible sites for future buildings; however, the plans were never executed. Instead, four houses for professors were authorized. Historians attribute the abandonment of the original plan to the financial constraints the university faced as a result of the [Panic of 1837](/wiki/Panic_of_1837 "Panic of 1837").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190631-59) Construction began in 1839, and in 1841, [Mason Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag"), the first campus building, was completed, followed by the construction of [South College](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag"), a nearly identical building to the south, in 1849, leaving a gap for a future grand centerpiece.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Colored_elevation_of_Mason_Hall.jpg/250px-Colored_elevation_of_Mason_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colored_elevation_of_Mason_Hall.jpg)

A colored elevation of [Mason Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1841–1950), the first instructional building on the Ann Arbor campus. Its design inspired [North Hall](/wiki/North_Hall_(University_of_Wisconsin) "North Hall (University of Wisconsin)") (1851) in [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin")

The first classes in Ann Arbor were held in 1841, with six freshmen and one sophomore taught by two professors, Joseph Whiting and George Palmer Williams. [Asa Gray](/wiki/Asa_Gray "Asa Gray") was the first professor appointed following the university's move to Ann Arbor in 1837.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDupree198867–68-60) He and the regents were both involved in stocking the [university library](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Library "University of Michigan Library").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmerHuntJune1906857-61) In 1846, Louis Fasquelle, a native of France, was appointed as the first professor of modern languages, primarily teaching French and writing textbooks.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuderstadt2006-62) French became the first modern language taught at the university. During the first commencement in 1845, eleven graduates, including [Judson Collins](/wiki/Judson_Dwight_Collins "Judson Dwight Collins"), were awarded Bachelor of Arts degrees.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWiley1858122-63)

After the university moved to Ann Arbor, the regents created several branches across the state,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussel1934-64) with the Detroit Branch School being the most significant. Advocated by R. C. Gibson in 1837, it opened in the renovated University Building on Bates Street in 1838,[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECatlin1923227-65) initially serving boys with one principal and one assistant.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889728–732-48) The school operated four terms a year, with its first public examination held in 1838.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarmer1889728–732-48) Many students were parishioners of Ste. Anne Church, and notable alumni included [Anson Burlingame](/wiki/Anson_Burlingame "Anson Burlingame") and [E. C. Walker](/wiki/E._C._Walker "E. C. Walker").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmerHuntJune1906674–709-46) Other branches were established in cities like [Pontiac](/wiki/Pontiac,_Michigan "Pontiac, Michigan"), [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan"), [Niles](/wiki/Niles,_Michigan "Niles, Michigan"), and [Tecumseh](/wiki/Tecumseh,_Michigan "Tecumseh, Michigan"), but struggled with enrollment, leading some to merge with local colleges, such as [Kalamazoo College](/wiki/Kalamazoo_College "Kalamazoo College"), which was affiliated with the University of Michigan from 1840 to 1850.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussel1934-64)

The administration during the early years of the university was complicated and designed to keep it tightly under state authority. The university's business was often intertwined with state affairs. The position of chancellor of the university, created by the organic act in 1837,[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190626-66) was never filled, and the positions on the board of regents, appointed by the governor, were often held by state officials. The [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Michigan "Lieutenant Governor of Michigan"), the justices of the [Michigan Supreme Court](/wiki/Michigan_Supreme_Court "Michigan Supreme Court"), and the chancellor of the state all served as ex officio members of the board, with the governor himself chairing the board. The regents' powers were shared with a rotating roster of professors, who were responsible for some vague aspects of the university's administrative duties; however, all important decisions had to be made by the governor and his party.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190617,_40-67) There were several attempts to gain independence from the state legislature, but progress was slow until the late 1840s, when the regents gained leverage, supported by Michigan citizens.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190640-68) This shift culminated in a revision of the organic act on April 8, 1851, which freed the university from legislative control, transitioned the regent positions from appointed to elected, and established a president selected by the regents.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190640-68)

### 1851–1900

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1851–1900")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/JasperCropseyUniversityOfMichigan.jpg/250px-JasperCropseyUniversityOfMichigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:JasperCropseyUniversityOfMichigan.jpg)

*University of Michigan* (1855) by [Jasper Francis Cropsey](/wiki/Jasper_Francis_Cropsey "Jasper Francis Cropsey")

[Henry Philip Tappan](/wiki/Henry_Philip_Tappan "Henry Philip Tappan") became the university's first president in 1852, with the ambition to shape the institution as a model for future universities. During his decade of service, he overhauled the curriculum,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) expanded the library and museum collections,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) established the law school, and supervised the construction of the [Detroit Observatory](/wiki/Detroit_Observatory "Detroit Observatory").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) He secularized faculty appointments by prioritizing merit in selections,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) breaking away from the retrograde tradition of regents distributing positions among Protestant denominations.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) In 1855, Michigan became the second university in the country to issue [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") degrees.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190648-70) The following year, the country's first chemical laboratory was built on campus, specifically designed for chemistry education, providing additional space for classes and laboratories. Tappan's tenure also saw the creation of the [Michigan Glee Club](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Men%27s_Glee_Club "University of Michigan Men's Glee Club"), the oldest student organization at the university, and the publication of the first student newspaper, *The Peninsular Phoenix and Gazetteer*, in 1857.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHumphrey1898136–139-71) Despite these accomplishments, Tappan's 11-year presidency was marked by considerable tension.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) His impartial stance on religion faced backlash during a time of heightened religious fervor.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) Due to changes in the Board of Regents and discontent with his administration, he was forced to resign in 1863.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Mason_Hall_1841.jpg/250px-Mason_Hall_1841.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mason_Hall_1841.jpg)

Mason Hall, with a statue of [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") in front. Photograph taken prior to 1870.

In 1863, [Erastus Otis Haven](/wiki/Erastus_Otis_Haven "Erastus Otis Haven") took office as president, having been a professor at the time and needing to prove his right for the presidency.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194254–59-72) The campus was divided by conflicting views among students, faculty, and regents regarding Tappan's restoration, the homeopathy crisis, and the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194254–59-72) Haven's administration faced routine administrative difficulties and struggled to garner support for increased state aid, despite achieving modest gains.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194254–59-72) The university, which had received a fixed $15,000 since 1869, still required additional funding.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194254–59-72) Frustrated, Haven resigned in 1869 to become president of [Northwestern](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"), a [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") institution, a move that sectarians viewed as a setback for secular colleges.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194254–59-72) The presidency remained vacant from 1869 to 1871, with Professor [Henry Simmons Frieze](/wiki/Henry_Simmons_Frieze "Henry Simmons Frieze") serving as acting president.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194259–62-73) During this period, the university raised funds for University Hall, overhauled admissions with a diploma system, and introduced coeducation.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194259–62-73) Women were first admitted in 1870,[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurmester20066–25-74)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockwell1988-75) although [Alice Robinson Boise Wood](/wiki/Alice_Robinson_Boise_Wood "Alice Robinson Boise Wood") was the first woman to attend classes in 1866.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuber1970-76)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBordin2001-77) In 1871, [Sarah Killgore](/wiki/Sarah_Killgore "Sarah Killgore") became the first woman to graduate from law school and be admitted to the bar of any state in the United States.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillardLivermore1893759-78) Frieze, a champion of music education, also established the [University Musical Society](/wiki/University_Musical_Society "University Musical Society").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194259–62-73) By the late 1860s, the university had become one of the largest in the nation, alongside Harvard in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"). However, it faced ongoing issues with student discipline, including class rushes, instances of hazing, and rowdiness in chapel.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194259–62-73) Frieze attributed these problems to a lack of centralized faculty control.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194259–62-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Bhl_BL004525_BL004525_bl004525.jpg/250px-Bhl_BL004525_BL004525_bl004525.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bhl_BL004525_BL004525_bl004525.jpg)

[University Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (center, 1872–1950), flanked by [Mason Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (left, 1841–1950) and [South College](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (right, 1849–1950), was located partly on the grounds of present-day [Angell Hall](/wiki/Angell_Hall "Angell Hall").

[James Burrill Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell") became president in 1871 and would remain in the post for nearly four decades.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) His tenure would be remembered as the most successful in the university's history.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Tappan's reforms in the 1850s set the university on a path to becoming an elite institution, but it was Angell who completed that transformation.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Shortly after Angell's arrival, [University Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") was completed at vast expense; it would remain the university's major academic building right up until the 1950s.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) During his presidency, Angell restored campus discipline,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) raised entrance and graduation requirements,[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) and persuaded the legislature to increase state aid.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Angell's tenure saw the addition of many extracurricular activities, including the [intercollegiate football team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Though a reformer, Angell was not authoritarian; he encouraged open debate and aimed for near-unanimous agreement before implementing changes, rather than pushing through with only a narrow majority.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) This approach enabled him to address knotty issues on campus, including the long-standing homeopathy problem.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Angell transformed the curriculum to focus on electives, expanding course offerings.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) That led to a faculty of great minds in many fields, from [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey") in philosophy to [Frederick George Novy](/wiki/Frederick_George_Novy "Frederick George Novy") in bacteriology.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1973xii–xix-80) In 1875, the university founded the [College of Dental Surgery](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Dentistry "University of Michigan School of Dentistry"),[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVedderBuntingWard192516-81) followed by the establishment of the [College of Pharmacy](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Pharmacy "University of Michigan College of Pharmacy") by [Albert B. Prescott](/wiki/Albert_B._Prescott "Albert B. Prescott") in 1876. That year, the university awarded its first [Doctor of Philosophy](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") degrees: to [Victor C. Vaughan](/wiki/Victor_C._Vaughan "Victor C. Vaughan") in chemistry and William E. Smith in zoology.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaughan192676–97-82)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale1906-83) They were among the first doctoral degrees to be conferred in the nation.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaughan192676–97-82)

> "Stand up for America; devote your life to its cause; love your homes, and prove as worthy of our cherished free institutions as they are worthy of your allegiance and service. Let not the high standard of National Honor, raised by the fathers, be lowered by their sons. Let learning, liberty and law be exalted and enthroned."

[William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley"), speaking to the first National Convention of the College Republicans in [Newberry Hall](/wiki/Newberry_Hall "Newberry Hall") in 1892[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStewart2002-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Literary_Class_of_1880.jpg/250px-Literary_Class_of_1880.jpg)](/wiki/File:Literary_Class_of_1880.jpg)

Literary Class of 1880 (includes [Mary Henrietta Graham](/wiki/Mary_Henrietta_Graham "Mary Henrietta Graham"), the first African American woman graduate of the University of Michigan)

With his presidency, Angell focused the university on preparing a new generation of statesmen for public service.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Angell himself was frequently called upon by the White House for diplomatic missions.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) In 1880, President [Rutherford Hayes](/wiki/Rutherford_Hayes "Rutherford Hayes") appointed him as Minister to China, where he successfully negotiated an immigration treaty that facilitated foreign student enrollment.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Later, in 1887, 1896, and 1897, President [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") appointed him to fisheries and waterways commissions.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) That same year, President [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") named him Envoy Extraordinary to Turkey.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) By the late 19th century, the university had gained an international reputation, in large part due to Angell's diplomatic efforts.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) At this time, over eighty subjects of the [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") were sent to Ann Arbor to study law as the empire opened to outside influence. Meanwhile, Michigan alumni such as [Dean Conant Worcester](/wiki/Dean_Conant_Worcester "Dean Conant Worcester") and [George A. Malcolm](/wiki/George_A._Malcolm "George A. Malcolm") helped shape the public institutions of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalata200290–91-85) Despite his extensive travels, Angell remained deeply connected to his students; campus lore has it that the aging president's memory was so sharp that he knew every student by name.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Angell retired in 1909, and seven years later, he died in the [President's House](/wiki/President%27s_House,_University_of_Michigan "President's House, University of Michigan"), which had been his home for forty-five years.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) His successor, [Harry Burns Hutchins](/wiki/Harry_Burns_Hutchins "Harry Burns Hutchins"), who was once his student, would lead the university through [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the [Great Influenza epidemic](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79)

### 1900–1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1900–1950")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Photo_of_courtyard_Law_Quadrangle.jpg/250px-Photo_of_courtyard_Law_Quadrangle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photo_of_courtyard_Law_Quadrangle.jpg)

Law Quadrangle, ca. 1930s

In 1910, [Harry Burns Hutchins](/wiki/Harry_Burns_Hutchins "Harry Burns Hutchins") assumed the presidency, becoming the first alumnus to hold that position.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) He had spent seven years in [Ithaca, New York](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York"), where he was called by [Andrew Dickson White](/wiki/Andrew_Dickson_White "Andrew Dickson White") and [Charles Kendall Adams](/wiki/Charles_Kendall_Adams "Charles Kendall Adams") to establish the [Cornell Law School](/wiki/Cornell_Law_School "Cornell Law School").[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) Hutchins then became the dean of the law school at his alma mater, where he introduced the [case method](/wiki/Case_method "Case method") of instruction.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) Hutchins was acting president when Angell was absent.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) During his presidency, Hutchins established the [Graduate School](/wiki/Rackham_Graduate_School "Rackham Graduate School"),[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) doubled enrollment,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) and increased the faculty.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) He secured more state aid and alumni support to fund the university's capital needs,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) including the gothic [Law Quadrangle](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School#Campus "University of Michigan Law School"), [Martha Cook Building](/wiki/Martha_Cook_Building "Martha Cook Building"), [Hill Auditorium](/wiki/Hill_Auditorium "Hill Auditorium"), and [Michigan Union](/wiki/Michigan_Union "Michigan Union"), which became campus landmarks. Hutchins enhanced the university health service,[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) but wartime distractions plagued his presidency.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) The influenza epidemic, which caused student deaths from poor care, deeply troubled him.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86) Well-liked by the regents who encouraged him to remain president, nonetheless, Hutchins retired in 1920.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194276–80-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/F._Earl_Christy_University_of_Michigan_Postcard.png/250px-F._Earl_Christy_University_of_Michigan_Postcard.png)](/wiki/File:F._Earl_Christy_University_of_Michigan_Postcard.png)

A 1907 postcard illustrated by F. Earl Christy that features a Michigan football theme

The 1920s at the university were marked by the brief tenures of two presidents, [Marion LeRoy Burton](/wiki/Marion_LeRoy_Burton "Marion LeRoy Burton") and [Clarence Cook Little](/wiki/Clarence_Cook_Little "Clarence Cook Little").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–98-87) In 1920, when Burton assumed office, a conference on higher education took place at the university, resulting in the establishment of the [Association of Governing Boards of Universities and Colleges](/wiki/Association_of_Governing_Boards_of_Universities_and_Colleges "Association of Governing Boards of Universities and Colleges").[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) Under his leadership, construction boomed on campus,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) and enrollments increased,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) propelled by the prosperous economy of the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"). He initiated the annual honors convocation,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) introduced the deans' conference,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) and increased university income.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) Burton fell ill in 1924 and died in 1925.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) In this emergency, President Emeritus Hutchins was called by the regents to assist, with [Alfred Henry Lloyd](/wiki/Alfred_Henry_Lloyd "Alfred Henry Lloyd") serving as acting president until Little's arrival.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194281–88-88) Clarence Cook Little was elected president in 1925.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194288–98-89) He was considered a polarizing figure due to his progressive stance, which alienated many [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics"). The proposal for establishing a nonsectarian [divinity school](/wiki/Divinity_school "Divinity school") on campus came after strong advocacy from [Charles Foster Kent](/wiki/Charles_Foster_Kent "Charles Foster Kent") and received unanimous backing from nearby churches.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaipson199593–123-90) However, this school was short-lived and was quietly shelved in 1927 during Little's presidency.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaipson199593–123-90) Little advocated for individualized education[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194288–98-89) and sought to reform curricula, particularly for women.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194288–98-89) He proposed a curriculum division after two years of study to address knowledge gaps, which led to the University College proposal.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194288–98-89) This initiative was ultimately abandoned following his resignation in 1929.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194288–98-89)

Michigan Symposium in Theoretical Physics, 1928 to 1941

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Goudsmit%2C_Heisenberg%2C_and_Fermi_at_Michigan_1939.png/330px-Goudsmit%2C_Heisenberg%2C_and_Fermi_at_Michigan_1939.png)](/wiki/File:Goudsmit,_Heisenberg,_and_Fermi_at_Michigan_1939.png)

[Samuel Goudsmit](/wiki/Samuel_Goudsmit "Samuel Goudsmit"), Clarence Yoakum, [Werner Heisenberg](/wiki/Werner_Heisenberg "Werner Heisenberg"), [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), and [Edward H. Kraus](/wiki/Edward_Henry_Kraus "Edward Henry Kraus") attending the symposium

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/People_attending_UM_physics_symposium_pose_for_group_photo_Summer_1931.jpg/330px-People_attending_UM_physics_symposium_pose_for_group_photo_Summer_1931.jpg)](/wiki/File:People_attending_UM_physics_symposium_pose_for_group_photo_Summer_1931.jpg)

[H.A. Kramers](/wiki/Hans_Kramers "Hans Kramers"), second row, sixth left with [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer"), second row, fourth left, in a photograph of the symposium

Following Little's resignation, [Alexander Grant Ruthven](/wiki/Alexander_Grant_Ruthven "Alexander Grant Ruthven"), an alumnus, was elected president by unanimous vote.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194298–116-91) He would lead the university through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194298–116-91) Under Ruthven's leadership, the university administration became more decentralized with the creation of the university council, various divisions, and a system of committees.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194298–116-91) For years, the university was a backwater in theoretical physics. Nonetheless, this changed under department head [Harrison McAllister Randall](/wiki/Harrison_McAllister_Randall "Harrison McAllister Randall"), who brought theorists [Oskar Klein](/wiki/Oskar_Klein "Oskar Klein"), [George Uhlenbeck](/wiki/George_Uhlenbeck "George Uhlenbeck") and [Samuel Goudsmit](/wiki/Samuel_Goudsmit "Samuel Goudsmit") onto the faculty.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraneDennison1979268–285-92) Goudsmit mentored famous students at the university, including [Robert Bacher](/wiki/Robert_Bacher "Robert Bacher") and [Wu Ta-You](/wiki/Wu_Ta-You "Wu Ta-You"), the Father of Chinese Physics, who in turn taught [Zhu Guangya](/wiki/Zhu_Guangya "Zhu Guangya") and two Nobel laureates, [Chen Ning Yang](/wiki/Chen_Ning_Yang "Chen Ning Yang") and [Tsung-Dao Lee](/wiki/Tsung-Dao_Lee "Tsung-Dao Lee"). From 1928 to 1941, the Summer Symposium in Theoretical Physics featured renowned physicists like [Niels Bohr](/wiki/Niels_Bohr "Niels Bohr"), [Werner Heisenberg](/wiki/Werner_Heisenberg "Werner Heisenberg"), [Paul Dirac](/wiki/Paul_Dirac "Paul Dirac"), and [Erwin Schrödinger](/wiki/Erwin_Schr%C3%B6dinger "Erwin Schrödinger"), with at least fifteen attendees being Nobel laureates or future laureates.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoudsmit1961178–182-93) [Wolfgang Pauli](/wiki/Wolfgang_Pauli "Wolfgang Pauli") held a visiting professorship at the university in 1931. [Stephen Timoshenko](/wiki/Stephen_Timoshenko "Stephen Timoshenko") created the first U.S. bachelor's and doctoral programs in engineering mechanics when he was a faculty professor at the university. In 1948, shortly after World War II, the Michigan Memorial Phoenix Project was established to honor the hundreds of lives lost from the university during the war.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin201640–46-94) Funded by numerous contributors, including the [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), the Phoenix Project operated the [Ford Nuclear Reactor](/wiki/Ford_Nuclear_Reactor "Ford Nuclear Reactor"), which established the nation's first academic program in nuclear science and engineering.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin201640–46-94)

### 1950–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1950–present")]

For most of the 19th and early 20th centuries, Michigan stood as the [sole university within the state](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Michigan "List of colleges and universities in Michigan"), and its alumni often dominated [state politics](/wiki/Politics_of_Michigan "Politics of Michigan"). However, by the mid-20th century, the landscape began to shift as new universities emerged, many of which were former [technical schools](/wiki/Technical_schools "Technical schools"), thereby threatening that status quo.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKerr2001174-95) When [Harlan Hatcher](/wiki/Harlan_Hatcher "Harlan Hatcher") took office as president in 1951, he was entrusted with securing the university's preeminence among the nation and world's burgeoning research institutions. This marked the beginning of a phase of post-war development, during which Hatcher oversaw the construction of North Campus, the founding of [Flint Campus](/wiki/University_of_Michigan%E2%80%93Flint "University of Michigan–Flint"), and the establishment of the [Dearborn Campus](/wiki/University_of_Michigan%E2%80%93Dearborn "University of Michigan–Dearborn"). The latter two of which have since evolved into fully accredited universities, under the auspices of the Board of Regents. Nonetheless, the tenures of Hatcher and his successor, [Robben Wright Fleming](/wiki/Robben_Wright_Fleming "Robben Wright Fleming"), were marked by a sharp rise in campus activism, linked to the [Civil Rights Movement](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement "Civil Rights Movement") and the [anti-Vietnam War movement](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War"). In 1963, a controversial set of [race-conscious](/wiki/Race-conscious_policy "Race-conscious policy") [admission policies](/wiki/College_admissions_in_the_United_States "College admissions in the United States"), collectively known as "[affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_at_the_University_of_Michigan "Affirmative action at the University of Michigan")", was first introduced.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2017462–489-96) These radical measures, originated by [Hobart Taylor Jr.](/wiki/Hobart_Taylor_Jr. "Hobart Taylor Jr."), aimed to boost Black student enrollment at elite universities.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2017462–489-96) In 1964, a group of faculty hosted the nation's first [teach-in](/wiki/Teach-in "Teach-in") against U.S. foreign policy in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), attended by thousands of students.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZaroulisSullivan198437-97)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson199926-98)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson2000183-99)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeBenedettiChatfield1990108-100) Meanwhile, Hatcher controversially dismissed three professors for their refusal to cooperate with [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy")'s [House Un-American Activities Committee](/wiki/House_Un-American_Activities_Committee "House Un-American Activities Committee") during his tenure.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon2000551-101) Hatcher's successor, [Robben Wright Fleming](/wiki/Robben_Wright_Fleming "Robben Wright Fleming") guided the university through a turbulent era of activism. In 1969, a year into Fleming's tenure, the [Weather Underground](/wiki/Weather_Underground "Weather Underground"), the militant group that famously declared war on the United States, was founded on campus. A year later, a strike organized by the [Black Action Movement](/wiki/Black_Action_Movement "Black Action Movement") resulted in the university agreeing to several demands for minority support.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant20078-102) In 1971, the [Spectrum Center](/wiki/Spectrum_Center_(community_center) "Spectrum Center (community center)") was founded as the nation's oldest collegiate [LGBT student center](/wiki/LGBT_student_center "LGBT student center"). Meanwhile, support among students for [marijuana legalization](/wiki/Marijuana_legalization "Marijuana legalization") was gaining traction on campus, as evidenced by the annual [Hash Bash](/wiki/Hash_Bash "Hash Bash") rally that began in 1972. Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, campus unrest began to diminish the university's academic standing, which had been ranked among the top five in the nation.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKerr2007-103) That standing started to decline during Fleming's tenure. Campus unrest persisted during [Harold Tafler Shapiro](/wiki/Harold_Tafler_Shapiro "Harold Tafler Shapiro")'s presidency, which began in 1980, fueled by controversies surrounding the university's [national defense initiatives](/wiki/Strategic_Defense_Initiative "Strategic Defense Initiative") and [foreign investments](/wiki/Disinvestment_from_South_Africa "Disinvestment from South Africa").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/School_of_Kinesiology%2C_Emily_Mathews_%2834410287145%29.jpg/250px-School_of_Kinesiology%2C_Emily_Mathews_%2834410287145%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:School_of_Kinesiology,_Emily_Mathews_(34410287145).jpg)

A celebratory dinner for the university's matriculating PhD students in 2017

President [James Duderstadt](/wiki/James_Duderstadt "James Duderstadt") would succeed Shapiro and remain president until 1996.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuderstadt2007-104) He supported campus growth and fundraising initiatives.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuderstadt2007-104) Duderstadt's successor, [Lee Bollinger](/wiki/Lee_Bollinger "Lee Bollinger"), oversaw the construction of the School of Social Work building and Tisch Hall, named after alumnus [Preston Robert Tisch](/wiki/Preston_Robert_Tisch "Preston Robert Tisch"). In 2003, the U.S. Supreme Court heard two cases regarding the university's affirmative action admissions: [Grutter v. Bollinger](/wiki/Grutter_v._Bollinger "Grutter v. Bollinger") and [Gratz v. Bollinger](/wiki/Gratz_v._Bollinger "Gratz v. Bollinger").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2017-105) In 2002, the university elected its first female president, [Mary Sue Coleman](/wiki/Mary_Sue_Coleman "Mary Sue Coleman"), by unanimous vote. Throughout her presidency, Michigan's endowment saw continued growth, accompanied by a major fundraising drive known as "The Michigan Difference".[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastillaHodgeson2017-106) The Coleman's administration faced labor disputes with the university's labor unions, notably with the Lecturers' Employees Organization and the Graduate Employees Organization.[[n 1]](#cite_note-107) In the early 2000s, the university faced declining state funding, prompting suggestions for privatization.[[n 2]](#cite_note-108)[[n 3]](#cite_note-109) Despite being a state institution de jure, it adopted private funding models.[[n 4]](#cite_note-110) A 2008 legislative panel further recommended converting it to a private institution due to its minimal ties to the state.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKelderman2009-111) [Mark Schlissel](/wiki/Mark_Schlissel "Mark Schlissel") succeeded Coleman in 2014. Before his firing in 2022, Schlissel expanded financial aid offerings,[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFitzgerald2017-112) enhanced international engagement, and raised student diversity.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2016-113) He also led initiatives in biosciences[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErickson2018-114) and the arts.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta2022-115) Schlissel's successor, [Santa Ono](/wiki/Santa_Ono "Santa Ono"), would serve a short and lackluster tenure amid the [national pro-Palestinian protests](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses")[[n 5]](#cite_note-116)[[n 6]](#cite_note-117)[[n 7]](#cite_note-118)[[n 8]](#cite_note-119)[[n 9]](#cite_note-120)[[n 10]](#cite_note-121) before his immediate resignation.[[n 11]](#cite_note-122) The presidency has remained vacant since then, with [Domenico Grasso](/wiki/Domenico_Grasso "Domenico Grasso") serving as the interim president.

### Historical links

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Historical links")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/HarryHutchins_JamesAngell_AndrewWhite.png/250px-HarryHutchins_JamesAngell_AndrewWhite.png)](/wiki/File:HarryHutchins_JamesAngell_AndrewWhite.png)

University presidents [Harry Burns Hutchins](/wiki/Harry_Burns_Hutchins "Harry Burns Hutchins"), left, and [James Burrill Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell"), center, with [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") founder [Andrew Dickson White](/wiki/Andrew_Dickson_White "Andrew Dickson White"), right, in a 1900s photograph

The University of Michigan in the 19th century was influenced by the transatlantic [Republic of Letters](/wiki/Republic_of_Letters "Republic of Letters"), an intellectual community that spanned Europe and the Americas.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) Key figures, such as [Henry Philip Tappan](/wiki/Henry_Philip_Tappan "Henry Philip Tappan"), were instrumental in aligning the university with the ideals championed by the intellectual community, including liberty, reason, and scientific inquiry.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194239–53-69) Alumni and faculty from Michigan, like [Andrew Dixon White](/wiki/Andrew_Dixon_White "Andrew Dixon White"), carried these ideals forward as they shaped other institutions.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeckham1994-123)[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedlander2016-124) Notably, Cornell alumni [David Starr Jordan](/wiki/David_Starr_Jordan "David Starr Jordan") and [John Casper Branner](/wiki/John_Casper_Branner "John Casper Branner") later introduced these concepts to [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") in the late 19th century.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeckham1994-123) Early university leaders like [James Burrill Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell") contributed to establishing other universities by sharing their insights.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw194263–75-79) Alongside [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot") of Harvard, Andrew D. White of Cornell, and [Noah Porter](/wiki/Noah_Porter "Noah Porter") of Yale, Angell was heavily involved in the early period of [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University") as an advisor to the trustees and recommended [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman") as the first president of the wealthy new foundation.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHawkins196015-125) [Clark Kerr](/wiki/Clark_Kerr "Clark Kerr"), the first chancellor of the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), referred to Michigan as the "mother of state universities."[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKerr2007-103) From 1865 to 1910, the university belonged to an elite group of institutions that significantly influenced the development of modern American higher education.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVeysey1965-126)

* [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California"): had its early planning based upon the University of Michigan.[[b]](#cite_note-127)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarsden1994134–140-128)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStadtman19707–34-129)
* [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"): Michigan alumnus Robert Ezra Park played a leading role in the development of the [Chicago School of sociology](/wiki/Chicago_school_(sociology) "Chicago school (sociology)"). The University of Chicago Laboratory School was founded in 1896 by John Dewey and [Calvin Brainerd Cady](/wiki/Calvin_Brainerd_Cady "Calvin Brainerd Cady"), who were members of the Michigan faculty.
* Cornell University: [Andrew Dixon White](/wiki/Andrew_Dixon_White "Andrew Dixon White") and [Charles Kendall Adams](/wiki/Charles_Kendall_Adams "Charles Kendall Adams"), the first and second presidents of Cornell, respectively, were members of the Michigan faculty. Cornell had its [Law School](/wiki/Cornell_Law_School "Cornell Law School") founded by Michigan alumni Charles Kendall Adams and [Harry Burns Hutchins](/wiki/Harry_Burns_Hutchins "Harry Burns Hutchins"). Six of the fourteen past presidents of Cornell University have had connections to the University of Michigan. [Edmund Ezra Day](/wiki/Edmund_Ezra_Day "Edmund Ezra Day"), the fifth president, was the founding dean of Michigan's business school. [Frank H. T. Rhodes](/wiki/Frank_H._T._Rhodes "Frank H. T. Rhodes"), the ninth president, spent three years as vice president of academic affairs at Michigan. [Martha E. Pollack](/wiki/Martha_E._Pollack "Martha E. Pollack"), the fourteenth president, served as provost at Michigan from 2013 to 2017. Additionally, [Jeffrey S. Lehman](/wiki/Jeffrey_S._Lehman "Jeffrey S. Lehman") received his graduate degrees from Michigan.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedlander2016-124)
* Harvard University: Michigan alumnus [Edwin Francis Gay](/wiki/Edwin_Francis_Gay "Edwin Francis Gay") was the founding dean of the [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School") from 1908 to 1919, instrumental in the school's planning.
* [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"): had its [pharmacology department](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine") established by [John Jacob Abel](/wiki/John_Jacob_Abel "John Jacob Abel"), an alumnus of Michigan.
* Massachusetts Institute of Technology: had its [Media Lab](/wiki/MIT_Media_Lab "MIT Media Lab") co-founded by Michigan alumnus [Jerome Wiesner](/wiki/Jerome_Wiesner "Jerome Wiesner"). [Nicholas Negroponte](/wiki/Nicholas_Negroponte "Nicholas Negroponte"), the co-founder and Chairman Emeritus of the Media Lab, has held a visiting professorship at the University of Michigan.
* [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"): Michigan alumnus [Henry Wade Rogers](/wiki/Henry_Wade_Rogers "Henry Wade Rogers") was instrumental in transforming Northwestern from a small cluster of colleges into a major, nationally recognized university. His wife, [Emma Winner Rogers](/wiki/Emma_Winner_Rogers "Emma Winner Rogers"), founded the Northwestern University Settlement Association.
* [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University"): [Alexander Winchell](/wiki/Alexander_Winchell "Alexander Winchell") and [Erastus O. Haven](/wiki/Erastus_O._Haven "Erastus O. Haven"), the first and second chancellors of Syracuse University, respectively, were members of the Michigan faculty.
* [University of Washington](/wiki/University_of_Washington "University of Washington"): [Charles Odegaard](/wiki/Charles_Odegaard "Charles Odegaard"), who served as president of the University of Washington from 1958 to 1973 and is credited with elevating its academic standing, was previously the dean of Michigan's College of Literature, Science, and the Arts.
* [Wellesley College](/wiki/Wellesley_College "Wellesley College"): Michigan alumna [Alice Freeman Palmer](/wiki/Alice_Freeman_Palmer "Alice Freeman Palmer"), the president of [Wellesley College](/wiki/Wellesley_College "Wellesley College") from 1881 to 1887, "transformed the fledgling school from one devoted to Christian domesticity into one of the nation's premier colleges for women."
* [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"): had its [residential college system](/wiki/Residential_colleges_of_Yale_University "Residential colleges of Yale University") co-organized by [James Rowland Angell](/wiki/James_Rowland_Angell "James Rowland Angell"), a graduate of Michigan.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBergin1983-130) Michigan alumnus [Henry Wade Rogers](/wiki/Henry_Wade_Rogers "Henry Wade Rogers") introduced the "case system" and the college degree requirement into the [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School").

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Campus")]
