University of Michigan campus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Richard_Rummel_lithograph_of_the_University_of_Michigan_Campus_in_1907.jpg/500px-Richard_Rummel_lithograph_of_the_University_of_Michigan_Campus_in_1907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_Rummel_lithograph_of_the_University_of_Michigan_Campus_in_1907.jpg)

A hand-colored lithograph depicting a view of the University of Michigan by [Richard Rummell](/wiki/Richard_Rummell "Richard Rummell") in 1907

The University of Michigan's campus in Ann Arbor is divided into four main areas: the Central Campus area, the North Campus area, the North Medical Campus area, and Ross Athletic Campus area. The campus areas include more than 500 major buildings, with a combined area of more than 37.48 million square feet (860 acres; 3.482 km2). The Central and Athletic Campus areas are contiguous, while the North Campus area is separated from them, primarily by the [Huron River](/wiki/Huron_River_(Michigan) "Huron River (Michigan)"). The North Medical Campus area was developed on Plymouth Road, with several university-owned buildings for outpatient care, diagnostics, and outpatient surgery.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

There is leased space in buildings scattered throughout the city, many occupied by organizations affiliated with the University of Michigan Health System. In addition to the University of Michigan Golf Course on Ross Athletic Campus, the university operates a second golf course on Geddes Road called Radrick Farms Golf Course. The university also operates a large office building called Wolverine Tower in southern Ann Arbor. The Inglis House is an off-campus facility, which the university has owned since the 1950s. The Inglis House is a 10,000-square-foot (930 m2) mansion used to hold various social events, including meetings of the Board of Regents, and to host visiting dignitaries. Another major off-campus facility is the [Matthaei Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Matthaei_Botanical_Gardens "Matthaei Botanical Gardens"), which is located on the eastern outskirts of the City of Ann Arbor.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

### Central Campus Historic District

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Central Campus Historic District")]

Further information: [University of Michigan Central Campus Historic District](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Central_Campus_Historic_District "University of Michigan Central Campus Historic District")

See also: [The Diag](/wiki/The_Diag "The Diag")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Northwest_corner_of_UM_campus_about_1873.jpg/250px-Northwest_corner_of_UM_campus_about_1873.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northwest_corner_of_UM_campus_about_1873.jpg)

The northwest corner of [The Diag](/wiki/The_Diag "The Diag") around 1873, showing the university's gate, the [Law Building](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1863–1950) and [University Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1872–1950)

The original Central Campus spanned 40 acres (16 ha), bordered by North University Avenue, South University Avenue, East University Avenue, and State Street. The master plan was developed by [Alexander Jackson Davis](/wiki/Alexander_Jackson_Davis "Alexander Jackson Davis").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruettner200349-132) The first structures built included [four Greek Revival faculty residences](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") in 1840, as well as [Mason Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1841–1950) and [South College](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1849–1950), which functioned as both academic spaces and dormitories.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruettner2003-133) Only one of the original faculty residences remains today; it has been renovated in the Italianate style to serve as the [President's House](/wiki/President%27s_House,_University_of_Michigan "President's House, University of Michigan"), making it the oldest building on campus. The [Chemical Laboratory](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag"), built by [Albert Jordan](/wiki/Albert_Jordan "Albert Jordan") in 1856 and operational until 1980, was notable for housing the nation's first instructional chemistry lab.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruettner2003-133) After the completion of the [Old Medical Building](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1850–1914) and the Law Building (1863–1950), an open space known as [The Diag](/wiki/The_Diag "The Diag") began to take shape. Among the notable structures on the original Central Campus were [University Hall](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1872–1950), designed by alumnus [E. S. Jennison](/wiki/E._S._Jennison "E. S. Jennison"),[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChapman1881-134) and the [Old Library](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1881–1918), designed by [Henry Van Brunt](/wiki/Henry_Van_Brunt "Henry Van Brunt"), who was also the architect behind [Memorial Hall](/wiki/Memorial_Hall_(Harvard_University) "Memorial Hall (Harvard University)") (1870) in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/University_of_Michigan_Old_Library.jpg/250px-University_of_Michigan_Old_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_Old_Library.jpg)

The [Old Library](/wiki/The_Diag#Former_buildings "The Diag") (1881–1918), designed by [Henry Van Brunt](/wiki/Henry_Van_Brunt "Henry Van Brunt"), had an addition built to the south in 1898. The portion constructed in 1883 was torn down in 1918.

The Central Campus today, however, bears little resemblance to its 19th-century appearance, as most of its original structures were demolished and rebuilt in the early 20th century. The rebuilt structures are primarily the work of [Albert Kahn](/wiki/Albert_Kahn_(architect) "Albert Kahn (architect)"), who served as the university's supervising architect during that period. In 1909, Regent [William L. Clements](/wiki/William_L._Clements "William L. Clements") became chairman of the Building and Grounds Committee, leading to Albert Kahn's growing influence in the university's architectural development.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDann2007-135) Clements, impressed by Kahn's work on his industrial projects and residence in [Bay City](/wiki/Bay_City,_Michigan "Bay City, Michigan"), awarded him multiple university commissions and appointed him as the university's supervising architect.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDann2007-135) The West Engineering Hall (1910), Natural Science Building (1915), and General Library (1920) were all designed by Kahn. During a period of limited construction funding, these structures exhibited a simple design with minimal ornamentation. However, Kahn's [Hill Auditorium](/wiki/Hill_Auditorium "Hill Auditorium") (1913), adequately funded by Regent Arthur Hill, features extensive Sullivanesque ornamentation and excellent acoustic design, which was rare for that period.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Front_of_Angell_Hall.jpg/250px-Front_of_Angell_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_of_Angell_Hall.jpg)

[James Burrill Angell Hall](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell_Hall "James Burrill Angell Hall") (1924) on Central Campus serves as a major academic building for the [College of Literature, Science, and the Arts](/wiki/College_of_Literature,_Science,_and_the_Arts "College of Literature, Science, and the Arts").

Beginning in 1920, the university received greater funding for construction projects, thanks to president Burton's fiscal persuasiveness with the legislature, propelled by a prosperous economy. This allowed campus buildings to be constructed in a grand manner. Kahn's Italian Renaissance [Clements Library](/wiki/Clements_Library "Clements Library") (1923),[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDann2007-135) Classical Greek [Angell Hall](/wiki/Angell_Hall "Angell Hall") (1924),[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131) and Art Deco [Burton Memorial Tower](/wiki/Burton_Memorial_Tower "Burton Memorial Tower") (1936) all feature unusual and costly materials and are considered some of his most elegant university buildings. The last of Kahn's university commissions was the Ruthven Museums Building (1928), designed in the Renaissance style.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

Other architects who contributed to the Central Campus include [Spier & Rohns](/wiki/Spier_%26_Rohns "Spier & Rohns"),[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes197924–25-136) who designed [Newberry Hall](/wiki/Newberry_Hall "Newberry Hall") (1888), Tappan Hall (1894), and the West Medical Building (1904); [Smith, Hinchman and Grylls](/wiki/SmithGroup "SmithGroup"), the architects of the Chemistry Building (1910) and East Engineering Building; and [Perkins, Fellows and Hamilton](/wiki/Perkins,_Fellows_%26_Hamilton_Office_and_Studio "Perkins, Fellows & Hamilton Office and Studio"), who designed University High School (1924). The [Michigan Union](/wiki/Michigan_Union "Michigan Union") (1919) and Michigan League (1929), completed by alumni [Irving Kane Pond](/wiki/Irving_Kane_Pond "Irving Kane Pond") and [Allen Bartlit Pond](/wiki/Allen_Bartlit_Pond "Allen Bartlit Pond"), house the university's various student organizations.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWineberg200443-137)[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChmuraConsolino2007-138) Alumni Memorial Hall, funded by contributions from alumni in memory of the university's Civil War dead, was completed by [Donaldson and Meier](/wiki/Donaldson_and_Meier "Donaldson and Meier").[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReadeWineberg1992-139) It was designated as the [University Museum of Art](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Museum_of_Art "University of Michigan Museum of Art") in 1946. [Tony Rosenthal](/wiki/Tony_Rosenthal "Tony Rosenthal") created the monumental cube *[Endover](/wiki/Endover "Endover")* in 1968.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlbeeHunter2000-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381164236.jpg/250px-Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381164236.jpg)](/wiki/File:Law_Quadrangle,_University_of_Michigan,_University_Avenue_and_State_Street,_Ann_Arbor,_MI_-_54381164236.jpg)

[Lawyers Club](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School#Campus "University of Michigan Law School") (1924), Law Quadrangle

The area just south of The Diag is predominantly Gothic in character, contrasting with the classical designs prevalent in many of Kahn's university buildings. The [Martha Cook Building](/wiki/Martha_Cook_Building "Martha Cook Building") (1915), completed by [York and Sawyer](/wiki/York_and_Sawyer "York and Sawyer"), [Samuel Parsons](/wiki/Samuel_Parsons "Samuel Parsons"), and [George A. Fuller](/wiki/George_A._Fuller "George A. Fuller") in 1915, draws inspiration from England's [Knole House](/wiki/Knole_House "Knole House") and [Aston Hall](/wiki/Aston_Hall "Aston Hall"). It was one of the university’s early women’s residences. York and Sawyer also designed the [Law Quadrangle](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School "University of Michigan Law School"), which features a flagstone courtyard by landscape architect Jacob Van Heiningan. The Lawyers' Club, part of the quadrangle, includes a clubhouse, dining hall, and dormitory, modeled after English clubs with an [Elizabethan-style](/wiki/Elizabethan_architecture "Elizabethan architecture") lounge and a dining hall inspired by the chapels of [Eaton](/wiki/Eaton_Hall,_Cheshire "Eaton Hall, Cheshire"). The Law Library's main reading room showcases craftsmanship from the [Rockefeller Church of New York](/wiki/Riverside_Church "Riverside Church"). Hutchins Hall, designed by alumnus [James Baird](/wiki/James_Baird_(civil_engineer) "James Baird (civil engineer)"), is named after Harry Burns Hutchins, the fourth president of the university. Following its completion, nearby buildings like the School of Education Building, by [Malcomson and Higginbotham](/wiki/Malcomson_and_Higginbotham "Malcomson and Higginbotham"), and Emil Lorch's Architecture and Design Building adopted Gothic elements reflecting the style of the Law Quadrangle and Martha Cook Residence.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

The Central Campus is the location of the [College of Literature, Science and the Arts](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Literature,_Science,_and_the_Arts "University of Michigan College of Literature, Science, and the Arts"). Most of the graduate and professional schools, including the [Law School](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Law_School "University of Michigan Law School"), [Ross School of Business](/wiki/Ross_School_of_Business "Ross School of Business"), [Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_School_of_Public_Policy "Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy"), and the [School of Dentistry](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Dentistry "University of Michigan School of Dentistry"), are on Central Campus. Two main libraries, [Hatcher Graduate Library](/wiki/Hatcher_Graduate_Library "Hatcher Graduate Library") and [Shapiro Undergraduate Library](/wiki/Shapiro_Undergraduate_Library "Shapiro Undergraduate Library"), as well as the university's many [museums](/wiki/Museums_at_the_University_of_Michigan "Museums at the University of Michigan"), are also on Central Campus.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

### North Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: North Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/University_of_Michigan_August_2013_057_%28Earl_V._Moore_Building%29.jpg/250px-University_of_Michigan_August_2013_057_%28Earl_V._Moore_Building%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_August_2013_057_(Earl_V._Moore_Building).jpg)

Earl V. Moore Building (1964), designed by [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen")

The North Campus area built independently from the city on a large plot of farmland—approximately 800 acres (3.2 km2)—that the university bought in 1952. Architect [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen") devised the early master plan for the North Campus area and designed several of its buildings in the 1950s, including the [Earl V. Moore School of Music Building](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Music,_Theatre_%26_Dance#Facilities "University of Michigan School of Music, Theatre & Dance").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter200032–39-141) The university's largest residence hall, [Bursley Hall](/wiki/Bursley_Hall "Bursley Hall"), is in the North Campus area. The North Campus Diag features a bell tower called [Lurie Tower](/wiki/Lurie_Tower "Lurie Tower"), which contains a grand carillon.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuderstadt200383-142)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOgden201715–22-143)

The North Campus houses the College of Engineering, the [School of Music, Theatre & Dance](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Music "University of Michigan School of Music"), the [Stamps School of Art & Design](/wiki/Penny_W._Stamps_School_of_Art_%26_Design "Penny W. Stamps School of Art & Design"), the [Taubman College of Architecture and Urban Planning](/wiki/Taubman_College_of_Architecture_and_Urban_Planning "Taubman College of Architecture and Urban Planning"), and an annex of the [School of Information](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Information "University of Michigan School of Information"). The campus area is served by Duderstadt Center, which houses the Art, Architecture and Engineering Library. Duderstadt Center also contains multiple computer labs, video editing studios, electronic music studios, an audio studio, a video studio, multimedia workspaces, and a 3D virtual reality room. Other libraries located on North Campus include the [Gerald R. Ford Presidential Library](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Presidential_Library "Gerald R. Ford Presidential Library") and the [Bentley Historical Library](/wiki/Bentley_Historical_Library "Bentley Historical Library").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

### Ross Athletic Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ross Athletic Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/University_of_Michigan_August_2013_258_%28Golf_Course%29.jpg/250px-University_of_Michigan_August_2013_258_%28Golf_Course%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_August_2013_258_(Golf_Course).jpg)

The University of Michigan Golf Course was designed by Scottish golf course architect [Alister MacKenzie](/wiki/Alister_MacKenzie "Alister MacKenzie") and opened in 1931.

Ross Athletic Campus is the site for the university's athletic programs, including major sports facilities such as [Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium"),[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadejToonkelPearson199779-144) [Crisler Center](/wiki/Crisler_Center "Crisler Center"), and [Yost Ice Arena](/wiki/Yost_Ice_Arena "Yost Ice Arena"). The campus area is also the site of the Buhr library storage facility, Revelli Hall, home of the [Michigan Marching Band](/wiki/Michigan_Marching_Band "Michigan Marching Band"), the Institute for Continuing Legal Education, and the Student Theatre Arts Complex, which provides shop and rehearsal space for student theatre groups. The university's departments of public safety and transportation services offices are located on Ross Athletic Campus.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

The University of Michigan Golf Course is located south of Michigan Stadium. It was designed in the late 1920s by [Alister MacKenzie](/wiki/Alister_MacKenzie "Alister MacKenzie"), the designer of [Augusta National Golf Club](/wiki/Augusta_National_Golf_Club "Augusta National Golf Club") in [Augusta, Georgia](/wiki/Augusta,_Georgia "Augusta, Georgia"), home of the [Masters Tournament](/wiki/Masters_Tournament "Masters Tournament").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

### Residential halls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Residential halls")]

Main article: [University of Michigan Housing](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Housing "University of Michigan Housing")

The University of Michigan's residential system can accommodate approximately 10,000 students. The residence halls are located in three distinct geographic areas on campus: the Central Campus area, The Hill (between the Central Campus area and the main medical campus) and the North Campus area. The largest residence hall has a capacity of 1,270 students, while the smallest accommodates 25 residents.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacInnes1979-131)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Governance")]

See also: [Board of Regents of the University of Michigan](/wiki/Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Michigan "Board of Regents of the University of Michigan") and [President of the University of Michigan](/wiki/President_of_the_University_of_Michigan "President of the University of Michigan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/William_W._Cook_Legal_Research_Building%2C_Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381399838.jpg/250px-William_W._Cook_Legal_Research_Building%2C_Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381399838.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_W._Cook_Legal_Research_Building,_Law_Quadrangle,_University_of_Michigan,_University_Avenue_and_State_Street,_Ann_Arbor,_MI_-_54381399838.jpg)

William W. Cook Legal Research Building at the Law School, showing the emblem of the University of Michigan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Michigan_University_Regents_75th_Anniversary_Celebration_June_27_1912.jpg/250px-Michigan_University_Regents_75th_Anniversary_Celebration_June_27_1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_University_Regents_75th_Anniversary_Celebration_June_27_1912.jpg)

Photograph of Michigan University Regents 75th Anniversary Celebration on June 27, 1912.   
*Standing* L-R: Frank B. Leland, John H. Grant, Shirley W. Smith, Harry O. Bulkey, [William L. Clements](/wiki/William_L._Clements "William L. Clements"), [Lucius Lee Hubbard](/wiki/Lucius_Lee_Hubbard "Lucius Lee Hubbard"), Benjamin Hanchett, Junius E. Beal   
*Seated* L-R: Luther L. Wright, [James B. Angell](/wiki/James_B._Angell "James B. Angell"), [Harry B. Hutchins](/wiki/Harry_B._Hutchins "Harry B. Hutchins"), Walter M. Sawyer

The University of Michigan is governed by the [Board of Regents](/wiki/Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Michigan "Board of Regents of the University of Michigan"), established by the Organic Act of March 18, 1837. It consists of eight members, elected at large in biennial state elections for overlapping eight-year terms. Before the Office of President was established in 1850, the University of Michigan was directly managed by the appointed regents, with a rotating group of professors responsible for carrying out day-to-day administrative duties.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190637-145) The [Constitution of the State of Michigan of 1850](/wiki/Constitution_of_Michigan#1850_Constitution "Constitution of Michigan") restructured the university's administration. It established the Office of the President and transitioned the Board of Regents to an elected body. The state constitution granted the Board of Regents the power to appoint a non-voting presiding president to lead their meetings, effectively elevating the board to the level of a constitutional corporation independent of the state administration and making the University of Michigan the first public institution of higher education in the country so organized. As of 2021–22, the Board of Regents is chaired by Jordan B. Acker (B.A. '06).

The Board of Regents delegates its power to the [university president](/wiki/President_of_the_University_of_Michigan "President of the University of Michigan") who serves as the chief executive officer responsible for managing the day-to-day operations of the university, that is, the main campus in Ann Arbor. The president retains authority over the branch campuses in [Dearborn](/wiki/University_of_Michigan%E2%80%93Dearborn "University of Michigan–Dearborn") and [Flint](/wiki/University_of_Michigan%E2%80%93Flint "University of Michigan–Flint") but is not directly involved in their day-to-day management. Instead, two separate chancellors are appointed by the president to serve as chief executive officers overseeing each branch campus. All presidents are appointed by the Board of Regents to serve five-year terms, at the board's discretion, and there are no term limits for university presidents. The board has the authority to either terminate the president's tenure or extend it for an additional term.

The presidency of the University of Michigan is currently vacant, with [Domenico Grasso](/wiki/Domenico_Grasso "Domenico Grasso") (PhD ‘87) serving as the interim president.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMastony2024-146) Following the immediate resignation of [Santa Ono](/wiki/Santa_Ono "Santa Ono"),[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordanZaniewski2022-147)[[n 12]](#cite_note-148) the university's former president, the [board of regents](/wiki/Regents_of_the_University_of_Michigan "Regents of the University of Michigan") named Grasso as the university’s interim president on May 8, 2025.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMastony2024-146) He will lead the university during the search for the next president.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMastony2024-146) [Laurie McCauley](/wiki/Laurie_McCauley "Laurie McCauley") has been serving as the 17th and current provost of the university since May 2022, and she was recommended by former president Santa Ono to serve a full term through June 30, 2027.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOno2023-149)

The [President's House](/wiki/President%27s_House,_University_of_Michigan "President's House, University of Michigan"), located at 815 South University Avenue on the Ann Arbor campus, is the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") and office of the University President. Constructed in 1840, the three-story Italianate President's House is the oldest surviving building on the Ann Arbor campus and a [University of Michigan Central Campus Historic District](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Central_Campus_Historic_District "University of Michigan Central Campus Historic District") contributing property.

#### Student government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Student government")]

The Central Student Government, housed in the [Michigan Union](/wiki/Michigan_Union "Michigan Union"), is the university's [student government](/wiki/Student_governments_in_the_United_States#University_of_Michigan "Student governments in the United States"). As a 501(c)(3) independent organization, it represents students from all colleges and schools, manages student funds on campus, and has representatives from each academic unit. The Central Student Government is separate from the University of Michigan administration.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Senior_parade%2C_commencement_day%2C_University_of_Michigan.jpg/250px-Senior_parade%2C_commencement_day%2C_University_of_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senior_parade,_commencement_day,_University_of_Michigan.jpg)

Photograph of the senior parade on commencement day, circa 1903

Over the years, the Central Student Government has led voter registration drives, changed a football seating policy, and created a Student Advisory Council for Ann Arbor city affairs.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShea2016-150) A longstanding goal of the Central Student Government has been to create a student-designated seat on the Board of Regents. In 2000 and 2002, students Nick Waun, Scott Trudeau, Matt Petering, and Susan Fawcett ran for the Board of Regents on the statewide ballot as third-party nominees, though none were successful. A 1998 poll by the State of Michigan concluded that a majority of voters would approve adding a student regent position if put to a vote. However, amending the composition of the Board of Regents would require a constitutional amendment in Michigan.

In addition to the Central Student Government, each college and school at the University of Michigan has its own independent student governance body. Undergraduate students in the College of Literature, Science, and the Arts are represented by the LS&A Student Government. Engineering Student Government manages undergraduate student government affairs for the College of Engineering. Graduate students enrolled in the Rackham Graduate School are represented by the Rackham Student Government, and law students are represented by the Law School Student Senate as is each other college with its own respective government. In addition, the students who live in the residence halls are represented by the University of Michigan [Residence Halls Association](/wiki/Residence_hall_association "Residence hall association"), which contains the third most constituents after Central Student Government and LS&A Student Government.[[w 1]](#cite_note-151)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Finances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Umichlawquadrangle.jpg/250px-Umichlawquadrangle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Umichlawquadrangle.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/MichiganLawQuad.jpg/250px-MichiganLawQuad.jpg)](/wiki/File:MichiganLawQuad.jpg)

The William W. Cook Legal Research Library and other buildings comprising the Law Quadrangle were built during 1923–33 and then donated to the university by [William Wilson Cook](/wiki/William_W._Cook "William W. Cook"). It was the university's most significant private gift at the time.

In the fiscal year 2022–23, the [State of Michigan](/wiki/State_of_Michigan "State of Michigan") spent $333 million on the university, which represents 3.03% of its total operating revenues of $11 billion.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152) The university is the second-largest recipient of state appropriations for higher education in Michigan for 2022-23, trailing [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University") ($372 million). The Office of Budget and Planning reports that [Michigan Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_Medicine "Michigan Medicine")'s auxiliary activities are the largest funding source, contributing $6.05 billion to the Auxiliary Funds, which accounts for 55.1% of the total operating budget.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152) [Student tuition and fees](/wiki/College_tuition_in_the_United_States "College tuition in the United States") contributed $1.95 billion to the General Fund, accounting for 11% of the total budget.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152) Research grants and contracts from the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/U.S._federal_government "U.S. federal government") contributed $1.15 billion to the Expendable Restricted Funds, accounting for 10.4% of the total budget.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152)

The university's current (FY 2022–23) operating budget has four major sources of funding:[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2025-153)

* General Fund money, which accounts for 25.4% of the operating budget, is derived from various sources: [student tuition and fees](/wiki/Tuition_payments "Tuition payments") ($1.95 billion or 75.2%), state support ($333 million or 12.8%), [sponsored research](/wiki/Industry_funding_of_academic_research "Industry funding of academic research") ($301 million or 11.6%), and other revenue ($8 million or 0.3%). It covers the costs of teaching, student services, facilities, and administrative support. The state's annual contribution to the school's operating budget was 3.03% in 2023 and does not cover intercollegiate athletics, housing, or Michigan Medicine.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152)
* Auxiliary Funds, which account for 58.2% of the operating budget, are sourced from self-supporting units and do not receive taxpayer or tuition support. These include [Michigan Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_Medicine "Michigan Medicine") ($6.16 billion), [intercollegiate athletics](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines") ($186 million), [student housing](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_student_housing "University of Michigan student housing") ($160 million), and student publications.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152)
* Expendable Restricted Funds, which account for 14.2% of the operating budget, are from providers who designate how their money is spent. Funding comes from research grants and contracts, [endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") payout ($305 million), and private gifts ($157 million). It pays for [scholarships](/wiki/Scholarships "Scholarships") and [fellowships](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow"); salaries, benefits and research support for some faculty; and research, programs and academic centers.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152)
* Designated Funds, which account for 2.2% of the operating budget, come from fees charged for and spent on experiential learning, programs, conferences, performance venues, and executive and continuing education.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChatasMcCauleyMiller2022-152)

#### Endowment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Endowment")]

The university's [financial endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment"), known as the "University Endowment Fund", comprises over 12,400 individual funds. Each fund must be spent according to the donor's specifications. Approximately 28% of the total endowment is allocated to support academic programs, while 22% is designated for student scholarships and fellowships. Approximately 19% of the endowment was allocated to Michigan Medicine and can only be used to support research, patient care, or other purposes specified by donors.[[w 2]](#cite_note-154)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit), the university's endowment, valued at $17.9 billion, ranks as the tenth largest among all universities in the country.[[r 4]](#cite_note-155)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordan2023-156) The university ranks 86th in endowment per student. The [law school](/wiki/Michigan_Law "Michigan Law")'s endowment, totaling over $500 million, has a significantly higher per-student value compared to that of its parent university. It ranks as the eighth wealthiest law school in the nation in 2022.

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Academics")]

### Teaching and learning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Teaching and learning")]

The university offers 133 undergraduate majors & degrees across the College of Engineering (18), College of Literature, Science, and the Arts (77), College of Pharmacy (1), Ford School of Public Policy (1), LSA Residential College (3), Marsal Family School of Education (3), Ross School of Business (1), School of Dentistry (1), School of Information (2), School of Kinesiology (3), School of Music, Theatre & Dance (16), School of Nursing (1), School of Public Health (2), Stamps School of Art & Design (2), and Taubman College of Architecture & Urban Planning (2). The most popular undergraduate majors, by 2021 graduates, were computer and information sciences (874), business administration and management (610), economics (542), behavioral neuroscience (319), mechanical engineering (316), experimental psychology (312).

The [Horace H. Rackham School of Graduate Studies](/wiki/Horace_H._Rackham_School_of_Graduate_Studies "Horace H. Rackham School of Graduate Studies") offers more than 180 graduate degree programs in collaboration with fourteen other schools and colleges.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202445–70-159) Nineteen graduate and professional degree programs, including the [juris doctor](/wiki/Juris_doctor "Juris doctor"), [master of business administration](/wiki/Master_of_business_administration "Master of business administration"), [doctor of dental surgery](/wiki/Doctor_of_dental_surgery "Doctor of dental surgery"), [master of engineering](/wiki/Master_of_engineering "Master of engineering"), [doctor of engineering](/wiki/Doctor_of_engineering "Doctor of engineering"), [doctor of medicine](/wiki/Doctor_of_medicine "Doctor of medicine"), and [doctor of pharmacy](/wiki/Doctor_of_pharmacy "Doctor of pharmacy"), are offered exclusively by the schools and colleges; Rackham does not oversee their administration.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202445–70-159) The university conferred 4,731 master's degrees, 892 academic doctorates, and 738 professional doctorates in 2021-22.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202454-160)

Admission to the university is based on academic prowess, extracurricular activities, and personal qualities. The university practices [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") admission for domestic applicants. It does not consider [legacy preferences](/wiki/Legacy_preferences "Legacy preferences").[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarnoy2024506-166) Admissions officials consider a student's [standardized test](/wiki/Standardized_test "Standardized test") scores, application essay and letters of recommendation to be important academic factors, with emphasis on an applicant's [academic record](/wiki/Transcript_(education) "Transcript (education)") and GPA, while stating an applicant's high school class rank is 'not considered'. In terms of non-academic materials as of 2022, Michigan ranks character/personal qualities and whether the applicant is a first-generation university applicant as 'important' in making first-time, first-year admission decisions, while ranking extracurricular activities, talent/ability, geographical residence, state residency, volunteer work, work experience and level of applicant's interest as 'considered'. Some applicants to Music, Theatre and Dance and some applicants to the [College of Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Engineering "University of Michigan College of Engineering") may be interviewed. A portfolio is required and considered for admission for Art, Architecture and the [Ross School of Business](/wiki/Ross_School_of_Business "Ross School of Business"). Submission of standardized test scores is recommended but not compulsory.[[n 13]](#cite_note-167) Of the 55% of enrolled freshmen in 2025 who submitted SAT scores; the middle 50 percent Composite scores were 1370–1530. Of the 18% of the incoming freshman class who submitted ACT scores; the middle 50 percent Composite score was between 32 and 34.

*[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* rates Michigan "Most Selective",[[w 3]](#cite_note-168) and [The Princeton Review](/wiki/The_Princeton_Review "The Princeton Review") rates its admissions selectivity of 96 out of 99.[[w 4]](#cite_note-169) Admissions are characterized as "more selective, lower transfer-in" according to the Carnegie Classification. Michigan received over 87,000 applications for a place in the 2023–24 freshman class, making it one of the most applied-to universities in the United States.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas20249–23-170) Of those students accepted to Michigan's Class of 2027, 7,466 chose to attend.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas20249–23-170)

Since the fall of 2021, the university has had the largest number of students in the state, surpassing [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University")'s former enrollment leadership.[[n 14]](#cite_note-171) Given the state's shrinking pool of college-age students, there is public concern that the university's expansion could harm smaller schools by drawing away good students. Some of the state's regional public universities and smaller private colleges have already seen significant declines in enrollment, while others face difficulties in maintaining enrollment figures without lowering admission standards.[[n 15]](#cite_note-172)[[n 16]](#cite_note-173)

The university experienced an unexpected surge in student enrollment for the 2023 academic year, having admitted more students than it could support. This [over-yield](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") situation has placed considerable strain on student housing affordability, heightened faculty members' workloads, and stretched resources thin. The university is now embracing a steady-state admissions management strategy aimed at maintaining a stable class size.[[n 17]](#cite_note-174)

The [Horace H. Rackham School of Graduate Studies](/wiki/Horace_H._Rackham_School_of_Graduate_Studies "Horace H. Rackham School of Graduate Studies"), the graduate school of the University of Michigan, received a total of 21,554 applications for admission into its doctoral programs for the 2024 admission year, encompassing the summer and fall terms. The school extended offers of admission to 2,586 applicants, representing 12.00% of the applicant pool. Subsequently, 1,102 of the offers were accepted, resulting in a yield rate of 42.61% for the academic year. Applicants may submit multiple applications to different doctoral programs and receive multiple offers, but can only matriculate into one program at a time. Doctoral programs that are not administered by Rackham are not included in the statistics.

The selectivity of admissions to doctoral programs varies considerably among different disciplines, with certain highly competitive fields exhibiting acceptance rates in the single digits. For instance, in 2023, the field of [business administration](/wiki/Doctor_of_Business_Administration "Doctor of Business Administration") admitted only 5.2% of its 519 applicants. Similarly, the field of sociology had a selectivity rate of 5.01%, selecting from a pool of 439 applicants. The field of psychology was even more competitive, with a selectivity rate of 4.1% out of 805 applicants. Other traditionally highly competitive fields are philosophy, public policy & economics, political science, and robotics.

In August 1841, the university first published its admission requirements for incoming freshmen. These criteria placed a strong emphasis on proficiency in [ancient languages](/wiki/Ancient_languages "Ancient languages"), particularly [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and Greek.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190633-183) Prospective students faced an examination process that assessed their knowledge across various subjects, including arithmetic, algebra, English grammar, geography, [Latin literature](/wiki/Latin_literature "Latin literature") (Virgil and Cicero's Select Orations), Greek literature (Jacob's or Felton's Greek Reader), [Latin grammar](/wiki/Latin_grammar "Latin grammar") (Andrews and Stoddard's), and Greek grammar (Sophocles's).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A decade later, the university made a significant change to its admission policy. In 1851, it introduced a more flexible approach by waiving the ancient language requirement for students not pursuing the traditional collegiate course and allowing admission without examination in classical languages for these students.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190644-184) This adjustment can be viewed as a prelude to [scientific education](/wiki/Scientific_education "Scientific education"), signaling a gradual shift from the classical curriculum to a more diverse and modern academic offering.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the early days of the university, admission requirements varied across different departments, and most admissions were based on referrals. However, in 1863, a standardized [entrance examination](/wiki/Entrance_examination "Entrance examination") was introduced, establishing a single set of qualifications for admission to all academic and professional departments.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190679-185) The university administration at the time praised the implementation of this entrance examination, recognizing its contribution to enhancing the admission process.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsdale190644-184)

#### Affirmative action

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Affirmative action")]

Main article: [Affirmative action at the University of Michigan](/wiki/Affirmative_action_at_the_University_of_Michigan "Affirmative action at the University of Michigan")

In 2003, two lawsuits involving the university's [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States") admissions policy reached the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") (*[Grutter v. Bollinger](/wiki/Grutter_v._Bollinger "Grutter v. Bollinger")* and *[Gratz v. Bollinger](/wiki/Gratz_v._Bollinger "Gratz v. Bollinger")*). U.S. President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") publicly opposed the policy before the court issued a ruling.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBush2003-186) The court found that race may be considered as a factor in university admissions in all public universities and private universities that accept federal funding, but it ruled that a point system was unconstitutional. In the first case, the court upheld the Law School admissions policy, while in the second it ruled against the university's undergraduate admissions policy.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrilliot2007-187) The debate continued because in November 2006, Michigan voters passed [Proposal 2](/wiki/Michigan_Civil_Rights_Initiative "Michigan Civil Rights Initiative"), banning most affirmative action in university admissions. Under that law, race, gender, and national origin can no longer be considered in admissions.[[n 18]](#cite_note-188) The university and other organizations were granted a stay from implementation of the law soon after that referendum. This allowed time for proponents of affirmative action to decide legal and constitutional options in response to the initiative results. In April 2014, the Supreme Court ruled in *[Schuette v. Coalition to Defend Affirmative Action](/wiki/Schuette_v._Coalition_to_Defend_Affirmative_Action "Schuette v. Coalition to Defend Affirmative Action")* that Proposal 2 did not violate the U.S. Constitution. The admissions office states that it will attempt to achieve a diverse student body by looking at other factors, such as whether the student attended a disadvantaged school, and the level of education of the student's parents.

### Student success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Student success")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381401338.jpg/250px-Law_Quadrangle%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_University_Avenue_and_State_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_MI_-_54381401338.jpg)](/wiki/File:Law_Quadrangle,_University_of_Michigan,_University_Avenue_and_State_Street,_Ann_Arbor,_MI_-_54381401338.jpg)

An archway leading into the Law Quadrangle from South University Avenue

The university is listed among the leading suppliers of undergraduate and graduate alumni to [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") tech firms. In 2015, the university ranked 6th on the list of top feeder schools for [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), which employed over 500 graduates at the time. The university ranked 10th on the list of top feeder schools for [Meta](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms").[[n 19]](#cite_note-189) Google and Meta remain the university's first and second top employers in 2024.

In 2022, [Michigan Ross](/wiki/Michigan_Ross "Michigan Ross") ranked 11th among all business schools in the United States according to Poets & Quants, with its MBA graduates earning an average starting base salary of $165,000 and an average sign-on bonus of $30,000.[[n 20]](#cite_note-190)

The [U.S. Department of Education](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Education "U.S. Department of Education") reports that as of June 2024, federally aided students who attended University of Michigan-Ann Arbor had a median annual income of $83,648 (based on 2020-2021 earnings adjusted to 2022 dollars) five years after graduation. This figure exceeds both the midpoint for 4-year schools of $53,617 and the [U.S. real median personal income](/wiki/Personal_income_in_the_United_States "Personal income in the United States") of $40,460 for the year 2021 adjusted to 2022 dollars. Federally aided bachelor's graduates from the university's largest program, computer and information science, which had over 950 students in the 2020-21 cohort, had a median annual income of $153,297 five years after graduation.[[c]](#cite_note-fn2-191)

According to data from the U.S. Department of Education, [law](/wiki/Law_degree "Law degree") tops the list of most valuable [first professional degrees](/wiki/Professional_degree "Professional degree") offered by the university when ranked by earnings potential in 2022, with its federally aided students earning a median salary of $197,273 five years after graduation. [Dentistry](/wiki/Dental_degree "Dental degree") ($158,677), [pharmacy](/wiki/Bachelor_of_pharmacy "Bachelor of pharmacy") ($142,224), and [medicine](/wiki/Medical_degree "Medical degree") ($134,187) follow behind in that order.[[c]](#cite_note-fn2-191)

The fields of [business administration](/wiki/Bachelor_of_business_administration "Bachelor of business administration") ($140,827), [economics](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Economics "Bachelor of Economics") ($108,627), [mathematics](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Mathematics "Bachelor of Mathematics") ($107,395), and [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") ($105,494) are among the bachelor's degree programs with the highest earning potential offered by the university. In 2022, the university's federally aided students in these programs were earning median salaries exceeding the $100,000 threshold five years after graduation. Additionally, various engineering disciplines such as [computer engineering](/wiki/Computer_engineering "Computer engineering") ($123,120), [aerospace, aeronautical, and astronautical engineering](/wiki/Aerospace_engineering "Aerospace engineering") ($113,025), [industrial engineering](/wiki/Industrial_engineering "Industrial engineering") ($109,239), [electrical, electronics and communications engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering") ($109,107), [mechanical engineering](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering") ($101,514), [chemical engineering](/wiki/Chemical_engineering "Chemical engineering") ($100,000) are among the top-earning majors. [Computer sciences](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Computer_Science "Bachelor of Computer Science") ($153,297) and [information science](/wiki/Information_science "Information science") ($125,257) also fall into this high-earning category.[[c]](#cite_note-fn2-191)

Among all first-time freshman students who enrolled at the university in fall 2017, 82.0% graduated within four years (by August 31, 2021); 10.2% graduated in more than four years but in five years or less (after August 31, 2021, and by August 31, 2022); 1.1% graduated in more than five years but in six years or less (after August 31, 2022, and by August 31, 2023). The percentage of undergraduate students from the fall 2022 cohort returning in fall 2023 was 98.0% for full-time freshman students.

### Libraries and publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Libraries and publications")]

Main articles: [University of Michigan Library](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Library "University of Michigan Library") and [University of Michigan Press](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Press "University of Michigan Press")

See also: [ProQuest](/wiki/ProQuest "ProQuest")

The [University of Michigan library system](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Library "University of Michigan Library") comprises nineteen individual libraries with twenty-four separate collections—roughly 13.3 million volumes as of 2012. The university was the original home of the [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR "JSTOR") database, which contains about 750,000 digitized pages from the entire pre-1990 backfile of ten journals of history and economics, and has initiated a book digitization program in collaboration with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). The [University of Michigan Press](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Press "University of Michigan Press") is also a part of the library system.

Several academic journals are published at the university:

* The Law School publishes *[Michigan Law Review](/wiki/Michigan_Law_Review "Michigan Law Review")* and six other [law journals](/wiki/Law_journal "Law journal"): The *[Michigan Journal of Environmental and Administrative Law](/wiki/Michigan_Journal_of_Environmental_and_Administrative_Law "Michigan Journal of Environmental and Administrative Law")*, *[University of Michigan Journal of Law Reform](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Journal_of_Law_Reform "University of Michigan Journal of Law Reform")*, *[Michigan Journal of Race & Law](/wiki/Michigan_Journal_of_Race_%26_Law "Michigan Journal of Race & Law")*, *[Michigan Telecommunications and Technology Law Review](/wiki/Michigan_Telecommunications_and_Technology_Law_Review "Michigan Telecommunications and Technology Law Review")*, *Michigan Journal of International Law*, and *[Michigan Journal of Gender and Law](/wiki/Michigan_Journal_of_Gender_and_Law "Michigan Journal of Gender and Law")*.
* The Ross School of Business publishes the *[Michigan Journal of Business](/wiki/Michigan_Journal_of_Business "Michigan Journal of Business")*.
* Several undergraduate journals are also published at the university, including the *Michigan Journal of Political Science*, *Michigan Journal of History*, *University of Michigan Undergraduate Research Journal*, the *Michigan Journal of International Affairs*, and the *Michigan Journal of Asian Studies*.

### Museums and collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Museums and collections")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of museums and collections at the University of Michigan](/wiki/List_of_museums_and_collections_at_the_University_of_Michigan "List of museums and collections at the University of Michigan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/University_of_Michigan_August_2013_192_%28Kelsey_Museum_of_Archaeology%29.jpg/250px-University_of_Michigan_August_2013_192_%28Kelsey_Museum_of_Archaeology%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_August_2013_192_(Kelsey_Museum_of_Archaeology).jpg)

Newberry Hall ([Kelsey Museum of Archeology](/wiki/Kelsey_Museum_of_Archeology "Kelsey Museum of Archeology"))

The university is also home to several public and research museums including but not limited to the [University Museum of Art](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Museum_of_Art "University of Michigan Museum of Art"), [University of Michigan Museum of Natural History](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Museum_of_Natural_History "University of Michigan Museum of Natural History"), [Detroit Observatory](/wiki/Detroit_Observatory "Detroit Observatory"), [Sindecuse Museum of Dentistry](/wiki/Sindecuse_Museum_of_Dentistry "Sindecuse Museum of Dentistry"), and the LSA Museum of Anthropological Archaeology.

[Kelsey Museum of Archeology](/wiki/Kelsey_Museum_of_Archeology "Kelsey Museum of Archeology") has a collection of Roman, Greek, Egyptian, and Middle Eastern artifacts. Between 1972 and 1974, the museum was involved in the excavation of the [archaeological site](/wiki/Archaeological_site "Archaeological site") of [Dibsi Faraj](/wiki/Dibsi_Faraj "Dibsi Faraj") in northern [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarperWilkinson1975319–338-192) The Kelsey Museum re-opened November 1, 2009, after a renovation and expansion.

The collection of the University of Michigan Museum of Art include nearly 19,000 objects that span cultures, eras, and media and include European, American, Middle Eastern, Asian, and African art, as well as changing exhibits. The Museum of Art re-opened in 2009 after a three-year renovation and expansion. UMMA presents special exhibitions and diverse educational programs featuring the visual, performing, film and literary arts that contextualize the gallery experience.

The University of Michigan Museum of Natural History began in the mid-19th century and expanded greatly with the donation of 60,000 specimens by [Joseph Beal Steere](/wiki/Joseph_Beal_Steere "Joseph Beal Steere") in the 1870s.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarndtSinopoli2017-193)

The university places an emphasis on research and on attracting influential academics to join its faculty.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermanowicz200923-201) It is a large, four-year, residential research university accredited by the [Higher Learning Commission](/wiki/Higher_Learning_Commission "Higher Learning Commission").[[w 6]](#cite_note-202)[[w 7]](#cite_note-203)[[w 8]](#cite_note-204) The four-year, full-time undergraduate program comprises the majority of enrollments and emphasizes instruction in the arts, sciences, and professions with a high level of coexistence between graduate and undergraduate programs. The university has "very high" research activity and the comprehensive graduate program offers doctoral degrees in the humanities, social sciences, and [STEM fields](/wiki/STEM_fields "STEM fields") as well as professional degrees in medicine, law, and dentistry. The university has been included on the list of [Public Ivies](/wiki/Public_Ivies "Public Ivies").[[d]](#cite_note-205)

#### Comprehensive rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Comprehensive rankings")]

The 2025-2026 *U.S. News & World Report Best Global Universities* report ranked the university 21st among world universities with a global score of 83.2.[[w 9]](#cite_note-206)

University of Michigan-Ann Arbor was ranked 26th among world universities in 2023 by the [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities"), based on the number of alumni or staff as [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_laureates "Nobel laureates") and [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medalists "Fields Medalists"), the number of highly cited researchers, the number of papers published in [Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)") and [Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)"), the number of papers indexed in the [Science Citation Index Expanded](/wiki/Science_Citation_Index_Expanded "Science Citation Index Expanded") and [Social Sciences Citation Index](/wiki/Social_Sciences_Citation_Index "Social Sciences Citation Index"), and the per capita academic performance of the institution.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 2024 edition of the [CWUR Rankings](/wiki/CWUR "CWUR") ranked the university 13th nationally and 16th globally, with an overall score of 89.1, taking into account all four areas evaluated by CWUR: education, employability, faculty, and research.[[w 10]](#cite_note-207)[[w 11]](#cite_note-208) This metric evaluates the number of faculty members who have received prestigious academic distinctions (10% weight). The university's employability ranking is 42nd globally, based on the professional success of the university's alumni, measured relative to the institution's size (25% weight). In the education category, the university is ranked 35th globally. This metric assesses the academic success of the university's alumni, measured relative to the institution's size (25% weight).

In the 2025 [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings"), University of Michigan-Ann Arbor was ranked 44th in the world, its lowest position in 10 years, with an overall score of 79.[[w 12]](#cite_note-209)[[n 21]](#cite_note-210)

The University of Michigan is one of the twelve founding members of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"), a consortium of the leading research universities in North America. The university manages the fourth-largest research budget of any university in the United States, with total R&D expenditures of $1.925 billion in 2023.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEForrest2010-219) The federal government was the main source of funding, with grants from the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"), [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), [Department of Defense](/wiki/Department_of_Defense "Department of Defense"), [Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy"), [Department of Transportation](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Transportation "United States Department of Transportation"), and [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") (NOAA), collectively accounting for over half of the research volume.

The first measurement of the [magnetic moment](/wiki/Magnetic_moment "Magnetic moment") and spin of free [electrons](/wiki/Electrons "Electrons") and [positrons](/wiki/Positrons "Positrons") was conducted by [H. Richard Crane](/wiki/H._Richard_Crane "H. Richard Crane"), an experimental physicist at the university. The university operated the [Ford Nuclear Reactor](/wiki/Ford_Nuclear_Reactor "Ford Nuclear Reactor") from 1933 to 1955, during which it conducted extensive research related to nuclear energy. It currently hosts several major research centers focusing on [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics"), reconfigurable manufacturing systems, and [wireless integrated microsystems](/wiki/Wireless_integrated_microsystems "Wireless integrated microsystems").

A pioneer in computing technology, the university designed and built the [Michigan Terminal System](/wiki/Michigan_Terminal_System "Michigan Terminal System"), an early time-sharing computer operating system,[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArdenGallerO'BrienWestervelt19661–16-220) and was involved in the development of the [NSFnet](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation_Network "National Science Foundation Network") national backbone, which is regarded as the foundation upon which the global Internet was built. In 2024, the university began collaborating with [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") on high-performance computing and AI research.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJordan2024-221)

The first inactivated [flu vaccine](/wiki/Flu_vaccine "Flu vaccine") was developed by [Thomas Francis Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_Francis_Jr. "Thomas Francis Jr.") and [Jonas Salk](/wiki/Jonas_Salk "Jonas Salk") at Michigan. This was the first of many advancements at the university related to vaccination, including the development of a [live attenuated influenza vaccine](/wiki/Live_attenuated_influenza_vaccine "Live attenuated influenza vaccine") by [Hunein Maassab](/wiki/Hunein_Maassab "Hunein Maassab").[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlockYogevHaydenAmbrose20084940–4946-222) The university also introduced [histotripsy](/wiki/Histotripsy "Histotripsy"), a non-invasive technique that uses focused ultrasound to treat diseased tissue, and has made significant contributions to medical technology with innovations such as the [EKG](/wiki/Electrocardiogram "Electrocardiogram") and [gastroscope](/wiki/Gastroscope "Gastroscope").

In the social sciences, the [Klein–Goldberger model](/wiki/Klein%E2%80%93Goldberger_model "Klein–Goldberger model"), an enhanced macroeconomic model, was developed by [Lawrence Klein](/wiki/Lawrence_Klein "Lawrence Klein") and [Arthur Goldberger](/wiki/Arthur_Goldberger "Arthur Goldberger") at the university.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpstein1987114–140-223) [George Katona](/wiki/George_Katona "George Katona") created [consumer confidence measures](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Consumer_Sentiment_Index "University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index") in the late 1940s. [J. David Singer](/wiki/J._David_Singer "J. David Singer") initiated the [Correlates of War](/wiki/Correlates_of_War "Correlates of War") project in 1963 to compile scientific knowledge about war.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinger1972243–270-224) The [American National Election Studies](/wiki/American_National_Election_Studies "American National Election Studies"), established with a National Science Foundation grant in 1977, has been based at the university and partnered with [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") since 2005.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Institute for Social Research](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Institute_for_Social_Research "University of Michigan Institute for Social Research"), established in 1949, is the nation's longest-standing laboratory for interdisciplinary research in the social sciences.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The university has been featured in multiple bibliometric rankings that assess its impact on academic publications through [citation analysis](/wiki/Citation_analysis "Citation analysis"). The [University Ranking by Academic Performance](/wiki/University_Ranking_by_Academic_Performance "University Ranking by Academic Performance") for 2023–24 has positioned the university at 16th globally.[[w 31]](#cite_note-239) Additionally, in 2024, the [Performance Ranking of Scientific Papers for World Universities](/wiki/Performance_Ranking_of_Scientific_Papers_for_World_Universities "Performance Ranking of Scientific Papers for World Universities") ranked it 13th worldwide.[[w 32]](#cite_note-240) The university has a significant presence in the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index"), ranking 6th nationally and 23rd globally among research institutions, with a share of 365.97 and a count of 1199 in 2022. The university boasted 28 researchers who were recognized by [Clarivate](/wiki/Clarivate "Clarivate") as being highly cited in 2023.[[w 33]](#cite_note-241) In 2019, the university had 120 faculty members who were national academy members, placing it 10th among its peers in this metric.

Undergraduate students participate in various research projects through the [Undergraduate Research Opportunity Program](/wiki/Undergraduate_Research_Opportunity_Program "Undergraduate Research Opportunity Program") (UROP) as well as the UROP/Creative-Programs.[[w 34]](#cite_note-242)

The university is a member of the international research association [Universities Research Association](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") and the state-wise [University Research Corridor](/wiki/University_Research_Corridor "University Research Corridor").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESerwach2008-243) Beginning in 2005, the university operated the UM-SJTU Joint Institute with [Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University"), but in 2025, it withdrew from the partnership due to national security concerns.[[n 22]](#cite_note-244)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Student life")]

### Student body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Student body")]
