As of fall 2023, the Ann Arbor campus had 52,065 students enrolled: 33,730 [undergraduate students](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education") and 18,335 [graduate students](/wiki/Graduate_student "Graduate student"). The total number of employees reached 53,831, which included 21,475 individuals working with [Michigan Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Medicine "University of Michigan Medicine"), 6,114 supplemental staff, 7,820 faculty members, and 18,422 regular staff.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDesprezPehlkeZichi2025-10)[[n 23]](#cite_note-245) The largest college at the university was the [College of Literature, Science, and the Arts](/wiki/College_of_Literature,_Science,_and_the_Arts "College of Literature, Science, and the Arts") with 21,973 students (42.2% of the total student body), followed by the [College of Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Engineering "University of Michigan College of Engineering") (11,113; 21.3%) and [Ross School of Business](/wiki/Ross_School_of_Business "Ross School of Business") (4,433; 8.1%). All other colleges each hosted less than 5% of the total student population.

Students come from all 50 [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and nearly 100 countries.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas20249–23,_45–70-246) As of 2022, 52% of undergraduate students were Michigan residents, while 43% came from other states.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas20249–23,_45–70-246) The remainder of the undergraduate student body was composed of international students.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas20249–23,_45–70-246) Of the total student body, 43,253 (83.1%) were U.S. citizens or permanent residents and 8,812 (16.9%) were international students as of November 2023.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorkin2023-247)

As of October 2023, 53% of undergraduate students self-identified as [White](/wiki/White_people "White people"), 17% as [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 7% as [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), 4% as [Black](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 5% as belonging to two or more races, and 5% as having an unknown racial composition. The remaining 8% of undergraduates were international students.

According to a 2017 report by the *New York Times*, the median family income of a student at Michigan was $154,000. 66% of students came from families within the top 20% in terms of income.[[n 24]](#cite_note-248) As of 2022, approximately 18% of undergraduate students received a [Pell Grant](/wiki/Pell_Grant "Pell Grant").[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202434-249)

### Groups and activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Groups and activities")]

By 2012, the university had 1,438 student organizations.[[w 35]](#cite_note-250) With a history of student activism, some of the most visible groups include those dedicated to causes such as [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") and [labor rights](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights"), such as local chapters of [Students for a Democratic Society](/wiki/Students_for_a_Democratic_Society_(2006_organization) "Students for a Democratic Society (2006 organization)") and United Students Against Sweatshops. Conservative groups also organize, such as the [Young Americans for Freedom](/wiki/Young_Americans_for_Freedom "Young Americans for Freedom").[[w 36]](#cite_note-251)

There are also several engineering projects teams, including the [University of Michigan Solar Car Team](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team"), which has placed first in the [North American Solar Challenge](/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge "North American Solar Challenge") ten times and podium in the [World Solar Challenge](/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") seven times and the Wolverine Soft student-run game studio, which has released more than 15 video games on [itch.io](/wiki/Itch.io "Itch.io") and [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)").[[w 37]](#cite_note-252) Michigan Interactive Investments,[[w 38]](#cite_note-253) the Tamid Israel Investment Group,[[w 39]](#cite_note-254) and the Michigan Economics Society[[w 40]](#cite_note-255) are also affiliated with the university.

The university also showcases many community service organizations and charitable projects, including Foundation for International Medical Relief of Children, Dance Marathon at the University of Michigan,[[w 41]](#cite_note-256) [The Detroit Partnership](/wiki/The_Detroit_Partnership "The Detroit Partnership"), [Relay For Life](/wiki/Relay_for_Life "Relay for Life"), U-M Stars for the Make-A-Wish Foundation, InnoWorks at the University of Michigan, SERVE, Letters to Success, PROVIDES, [Circle K](/wiki/Circle_K_International "Circle K International"), [Habitat for Humanity](/wiki/Habitat_for_Humanity "Habitat for Humanity"),[[w 42]](#cite_note-257) and Ann Arbor Reaching Out. [Intramural sports](/wiki/Intramural_sports "Intramural sports") are popular, and there are recreation facilities for each of the three campuses.[[w 43]](#cite_note-258)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Michigan_Union_2009.JPG/250px-Michigan_Union_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Union_2009.JPG)

[Michigan Union](/wiki/Michigan_Union "Michigan Union"), a [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") building constructed on land wholly owned by the student society in 1917, was designed by Michigan alumni [Irving Kane Pond](/wiki/Irving_Kane_Pond "Irving Kane Pond") and [Allen Bartlit Pond](/wiki/Allen_Bartlit_Pond "Allen Bartlit Pond").

The Michigan Union and Michigan League are student activity centers located on Central Campus; Pierpont Commons is on North Campus. The Michigan Union houses a majority of student groups, including the student government. The William Monroe Trotter House, located east of Central Campus, is a multicultural student center operated by the university's Office of Multi-Ethnic Student Affairs.[[w 44]](#cite_note-259) The University Activities Center (UAC) is a student-run programming organization and is composed of 14 committees.[[w 45]](#cite_note-260) Each group involves students in the planning and execution of a variety of events both on and off campus.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/CLyres19.jpg/250px-CLyres19.jpg)](/wiki/File:CLyres19.jpg)

The Compulsive Lyres performing at the [2019 ICCA Quarterfinals](/wiki/International_Championship_of_Collegiate_A_Cappella "International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/The_team_poses_for_a_group_photo_at_the_Bridgestone_World_Solar_Challenge_%2717_starting_line..jpg/250px-The_team_poses_for_a_group_photo_at_the_Bridgestone_World_Solar_Challenge_%2717_starting_line..jpg)](/wiki/File:The_team_poses_for_a_group_photo_at_the_Bridgestone_World_Solar_Challenge_%2717_starting_line..jpg)

The Solar Car Team team poses for a group photo at the [Bridgestone World Solar Challenge 2017](/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") starting line.

The [Michigan Marching Band](/wiki/Michigan_Marching_Band "Michigan Marching Band"), composed of more than 350 students from almost all of U-M's schools, is the university's [marching band](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band"). Over 125 years old (with a first performance in 1897), the band performs at every home football game and travels to at least one away game a year. The student-run and led [University of Michigan Pops Orchestra](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Pops_Orchestra "University of Michigan Pops Orchestra") is another musical ensemble that attracts students from all academic backgrounds. It performs regularly in the [Michigan Theater](/wiki/Michigan_Theater_(Ann_Arbor) "Michigan Theater (Ann Arbor)"). The [University of Michigan Men's Glee Club](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Men%27s_Glee_Club "University of Michigan Men's Glee Club"), founded in 1859 and the [second oldest](/wiki/Glee_club#Oldest_United_States_collegiate_glee_clubs "Glee club") such group in the country, is a men's chorus with over 100 members.[[n 25]](#cite_note-261) Its eight-member subset [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") group, the [University of Michigan Friars](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Friars "University of Michigan Friars"), which was founded in 1955, is the oldest currently running *a cappella* group on campus.[[w 46]](#cite_note-262) The University of Michigan is also home to over twenty other a cappella groups, including Amazin' Blue, The Michigan G-Men, and [Compulsive Lyres](/wiki/Compulsive_Lyres "Compulsive Lyres"), all of which have competed at the International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella (ICCA) finals in New York City. The Michigan G-Men are one of only six groups in the country to compete at ICCA finals four times, one of only two TTBB ensembles to do so, and placed third at the competition in 2015. Amazin' Blue placed fourth at ICCA finals in 2017.

The University of Michigan also has over 380 cultural and ethnic student organizations on campus. These range the Arab Student Association to Persian Student Association to African Students Association to even the Egyptian Student Association.[[w 47]](#cite_note-263)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Delta_Sigma_Delta_Fraternity%2C_1502_Hill_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Delta_Sigma_Delta_Fraternity%2C_1502_Hill_Street%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Delta_Sigma_Delta_Fraternity,_1502_Hill_Street,_Ann_Arbor,_Michigan_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Delta Sigma Delta](/wiki/Delta_Sigma_Delta "Delta Sigma Delta"), the first dental fraternity in the world

[Fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") play a role in the university's social life; approximately seven percent of undergraduate men and 16% of undergraduate women are active in the Greek system. Four different Greek councils—the Interfraternity Council, Multicultural Greek Council, [National Pan-Hellenic Council](/wiki/National_Pan-Hellenic_Council "National Pan-Hellenic Council"), and Panhellenic Association—represent most Greek organizations. Each council has a different recruitment process. National honor societies such as [Phi Beta Kappa](/wiki/Phi_Beta_Kappa "Phi Beta Kappa"), [Phi Kappa Phi](/wiki/Phi_Kappa_Phi "Phi Kappa Phi"), and [Tau Beta Pi](/wiki/Tau_Beta_Pi "Tau Beta Pi") have chapters at U-M.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Phi_Delta_Phi_Fraternity_House%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_Michigan.JPG/250px-Phi_Delta_Phi_Fraternity_House%2C_University_of_Michigan%2C_Ann_Arbor%2C_Michigan.JPG)](/wiki/File:Phi_Delta_Phi_Fraternity_House,_University_of_Michigan,_Ann_Arbor,_Michigan.JPG)

[Phi Delta Phi](/wiki/Phi_Delta_Phi "Phi Delta Phi"), the oldest legal organization in continuous existence in the United States

The university hosts three secret societies: Michigauma, Adara, and the Vulcans. Michigauma and Adara were once under the umbrella group "The Tower Society", the name referring to their historical locations in the Michigan Union tower. Michigauma was all-male while Adara was all-female, although both later became co-ed.

* [Michigauma](/wiki/Order_of_Angell "Order of Angell"), more recently known as the Order of Angell, was formed in 1902 by a group of seniors in coordination with university president James Burrill Angell. The group disbanded itself in 2021 due to public concerns about elitism and the society's history. The group was granted a lease for the top floor of the Michigan Union tower in 1932, which they referred to as the "tomb", but the society vacated the space in 2000. Until more recent reforms, the group's rituals were inspired by the culture of [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_of_the_United_States "Native Americans of the United States"). Some factions on campus identified Michigauma as a [secret society](/wiki/Secret_society "Secret society"), but many disputed that characterization, as its member list has been published some years in *[The Michigan Daily](/wiki/The_Michigan_Daily "The Michigan Daily")* and the *[Michiganensian](/wiki/Michiganensian "Michiganensian")*, and online since 2006 reforms.
* Adara, known as Phoenix, was formed in the late 1970s by women leaders on campus and disbanded itself in 2021 amid campus criticisms of secret societies. In the early 1980s they joined the tower society and occupied the sixth floor of the tower just below Michigamua.
* Vulcans occupied the fifth floor of the Union tower though were not formally a part of the tower society. They draw their heritage from the Roman god [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan_(mythology) "Vulcan (mythology)"). The group which used to do its tapping publicly is known for its long black robes and for its financial contributions of the College of Engineering.

### Media and publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Media and publications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/University_of_Michigan_Student_Publications_Building.jpg/250px-University_of_Michigan_Student_Publications_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_Student_Publications_Building.jpg)

Stanford Lipsey Student Publications Building

The [student newspaper](/wiki/Student_newspaper "Student newspaper") is *[The Michigan Daily](/wiki/The_Michigan_Daily "The Michigan Daily")*, which was founded in 1890 and is editorially and financially independent from the university. *The Daily* publishes daily online content and a weekly print edition. The [yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook "Yearbook") is the *[Michiganensian](/wiki/Michiganensian "Michiganensian")*, founded in 1896. Other student publications at the university include the conservative *[Michigan Review](/wiki/The_Michigan_Review "The Michigan Review")* and the *[Gargoyle Humor Magazine](/wiki/Gargoyle_Humor_Magazine "Gargoyle Humor Magazine")*.

[WCBN-FM](/wiki/WCBN-FM "WCBN-FM") (88.3 FM) is the student-run [college radio](/wiki/College_radio "College radio") station which plays in [freeform format](/wiki/Freeform_(radio_format) "Freeform (radio format)"). WOLV-TV is the student-run television station that is primarily shown on the university's cable television system. WJJX was previously the school's student-run radio station. A [carrier current](/wiki/Carrier_current "Carrier current") station, it was launched in 1953.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithWoodman201989-264)

### Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Safety")]

Violent crime is rare on campus, though there have been a few notorious cases, including [Theodore Kaczynski](/wiki/Theodore_Kaczynski "Theodore Kaczynski")'s attempted murder of professor [James V. McConnell](/wiki/James_V._McConnell "James V. McConnell") and research assistant Nicklaus Suino in 1985. In 2022, [David DePape](/wiki/David_DePape "David DePape"), the man convicted of attacking [Paul Pelosi](/wiki/Paul_Pelosi "Paul Pelosi"), the husband of former U.S. House Speaker [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi"), targeted [Gayle Rubin](/wiki/Gayle_Rubin "Gayle Rubin"), an associate professor of anthropology and women’s studies at the university. DePape testified during his trial that he hoped to use Nancy and Paul Pelosi in an effort to get to Gayle Rubin.[[n 26]](#cite_note-265)

In 2014, the University of Michigan was named one of 55 higher education institutions under investigation by the Office of Civil Rights "for possible violations of federal law over the handling of sexual violence and harassment complaints." President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s [White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault](/wiki/White_House_Task_Force_to_Protect_Students_from_Sexual_Assault "White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault") was organized for such investigations. Seven years later, in 2021, the university attracted national attention when a report commissioned by the university was released that detailed an investigation into sexual assault allegations against doctor [Robert Anderson](/wiki/Robert_Anderson_(sex_offender) "Robert Anderson (sex offender)") who reportedly abused at least 950 university students, many of whom were athletes, from 1966 to 2003.[[n 27]](#cite_note-266) Schembechler reportedly punched his then 10-year-old son Matthew after he reported abuse by Anderson.[[n 28]](#cite_note-267) Following the exposure of a similar history of abuse at [Ohio State University](/wiki/Ohio_State_University "Ohio State University"), male survivors of both Anderson at Michigan and Strauss at Ohio State spoke out to combat sexual abuse.[[n 29]](#cite_note-268) The University of Michigan settled with the survivors for $490 million.[[n 30]](#cite_note-269)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Michigan Wolverines](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines "Michigan Wolverines")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Burgee_of_University_of_Michigan.SVG/250px-Burgee_of_University_of_Michigan.SVG.png)](/wiki/File:Burgee_of_University_of_Michigan.SVG)

Burgee of University of Michigan

The university has 27 varsity intercollegiate sports, including 13 men's teams and 14 women's teams. Its intercollegiate sports teams participate in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") in most sports, with the exception of the [women's water polo team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_women%27s_water_polo "Michigan Wolverines women's water polo"), which competes in the [Collegiate Water Polo Association](/wiki/Collegiate_Water_Polo_Association "Collegiate Water Polo Association"). The teams compete at the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level in all sports, including Division I [FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") in football.

The teams share the nickname "Wolverines" with several other collegiate athletic teams in the country, such as the [Utah Valley Wolverines](/wiki/Utah_Valley_Wolverines "Utah Valley Wolverines"), the [Grove City Wolverines](/wiki/Grove_City_College "Grove City College"), and the [Morris Brown Wolverines](/wiki/Morris_Brown_College "Morris Brown College").

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/1886_University_of_Michigan_baseball_team.jpg/250px-1886_University_of_Michigan_baseball_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:1886_University_of_Michigan_baseball_team.jpg)

1886 Michigan baseball team

The university's athletic history dates back to the mid-19th century, beginning with the founding of the Pioneer Cricket Club in 1860.[[n 31]](#cite_note-270) Varsity sports began in 1866 with the formation of the [baseball team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_baseball "Michigan Wolverines baseball"), followed by the [football team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") in 1879 and the [men's tennis team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_tennis "Michigan Wolverines men's tennis") in 1893.

In 1896, the university became a founding member of the [Intercollegiate Conference of Faculty Representatives](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Conference_of_Faculty_Representatives "Intercollegiate Conference of Faculty Representatives"), which later evolved into the Western Conference (1896–1899) and eventually became known as the Big Ten Conference (since 1950).

In 1905, the university found itself at the center of a national controversy regarding violence and professionalism in college football, which sparked discussions about potentially banning the sport from college campuses. That fall, [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") President [David Starr Jordan](/wiki/David_Starr_Jordan "David Starr Jordan") wrote a series of articles in *[Collier's](/wiki/Collier%27s "Collier's")*, accusing various universities, including Michigan and its rival [Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), of engaging in "professionalism."[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatterson200084–87-271) He labeled coach [Fielding Yost](/wiki/Fielding_Yost "Fielding Yost") as the "czar of Michigan's system" and claimed he was traveling across the country "soliciting expert players" who were not true [student athletes](/wiki/Student_athletes "Student athletes"). In response, Michigan President [James Burrill Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell") called for a reform conference on football and appointed [Albert Pattengill](/wiki/Albert_Pattengill "Albert Pattengill") to represent Michigan at the event.[[n 32]](#cite_note-272)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Chicago-Michigan_football_program_%281897%29.jpg/250px-Chicago-Michigan_football_program_%281897%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago-Michigan_football_program_(1897).jpg)

Souvenir program from 1897 for the [Chicago–Michigan football rivalry](/wiki/Chicago%E2%80%93Michigan_football_rivalry "Chicago–Michigan football rivalry") game

The 1906 Angell Conference in Chicago led to several reform resolutions, including placing faculty in charge of gate receipts,[[n 33]](#cite_note-273) banning summer training and the "training table," and capping admission prices for college athletic events at fifty cents.[[n 34]](#cite_note-274) A key resolution targeted Michigan's highly paid football coach, Fielding Yost, by prohibiting the hiring of professional coaches. Michigan was expelled from the Big Nine Conference in April 1907 for noncompliance, partly due to the Yost Resolution.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatterson200084–87-271) The university rejoined the conference in 1917 after a nine-year absence.

In 1926, [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_football "Harvard Crimson football") made an agreement to play football against Michigan, dropping [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football") from its schedule due to past rough matches.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESynott1976188–202-275) Princeton perceived this move as a threat to the '[Big Three](/wiki/Big_Three_(colleges) "Big Three (colleges)")' relationship, fearing it would lose its status as a rival to Harvard and be relegated to a secondary class.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESynott1976188–202-275) By the 1930s, the 'Big Three' was restored and expanded into the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") in 1939.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESynott1976188–202-275)

In 2023, during the NCAA's investigation into [sign-stealing allegations](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_football_sign-stealing_scandal "University of Michigan football sign-stealing scandal") against the football team's staff members, the university's board of regents considered the possibility of leaving the Big Ten Conference due to dissatisfaction with the conference's handling of the investigation.[[n 35]](#cite_note-276) In 2025, the university again raised the possibility of leaving the Big Ten Conference to become [independent](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FBS_independent_schools "NCAA Division I FBS independent schools"), opposing the Big Ten's proposal for a media rights sale. Regent Jordan Acker stated that if Big Ten intended to move forward with the deal without the university, it "would be the end of Michigan, as far as I can see, in the Big Ten Conference."[[n 36]](#cite_note-277)

### Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Venues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Eastern_Michigan_vs._Michigan_2011_02_%28Eastern_on_offense%29.jpg/250px-Eastern_Michigan_vs._Michigan_2011_02_%28Eastern_on_offense%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastern_Michigan_vs._Michigan_2011_02_(Eastern_on_offense).jpg)

Michigan vs. [Eastern Michigan](/wiki/Eastern_Michigan_Eagles_football "Eastern Michigan Eagles football") football, 2011

The [Ray Fisher Stadium](/wiki/Ray_Fisher_Stadium "Ray Fisher Stadium"), constructed in 1923, serves as the home venue for the [baseball team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_baseball "Michigan Wolverines baseball"). The [Alumni Field at Carol Hutchins Stadium](/wiki/Alumni_Field_at_Carol_Hutchins_Stadium "Alumni Field at Carol Hutchins Stadium"), formerly known as the Varsity Diamond, is the home field for the university's [softball team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_softball "Michigan Wolverines softball"). The [Yost Ice Arena](/wiki/Yost_Ice_Arena "Yost Ice Arena"), opened in 1923, is the home arena for the [men's ice hockey team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_ice_hockey "Michigan Wolverines men's ice hockey"). The [Crisler Center](/wiki/Crisler_Center "Crisler Center"), opened in 1967 and previously known as the University Events Building and Crisler Arena, serves as the home venue for the men's and women's basketball teams as well as the women's gymnastics team. The [Phyllis Ocker Field](/wiki/Phyllis_Ocker_Field "Phyllis Ocker Field"), constructed in 1995 and built partially on the site of Regents Field, is the home venue for the university's field hockey teams.

[Michigan Stadium](/wiki/Michigan_Stadium "Michigan Stadium") is the [largest stadium in the United States](/wiki/List_of_U.S._stadiums_by_capacity "List of U.S. stadiums by capacity"),[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuderstadtWomack2004199-278), and ranks [third globally](/wiki/List_of_stadiums_by_capacity "List of stadiums by capacity").[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClotfelter2019115-279) Prior to the construction of Michigan Stadium in 1927, the football team played their home games at [Regents Field](/wiki/Regents_Field "Regents Field"). In 1902, [Dexter M. Ferry](/wiki/Dexter_M._Ferry "Dexter M. Ferry") donated land adjacent to Regents Field, and the entire complex was renamed [Ferry Field](/wiki/Ferry_Field "Ferry Field"). Ferry Field served as the home stadium for the football team until the opening of Michigan Stadium. Today, Ferry Field serves as a tailgating space for Michigan Stadium during football games.

### Fight songs and chants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Fight songs and chants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Varsity_%28sheet_music%29.png/250px-Varsity_%28sheet_music%29.png)](/wiki/File:Varsity_(sheet_music).png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/The_Victors_%28sheet_music%29.jpg/250px-The_Victors_%28sheet_music%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Victors_(sheet_music).jpg)

Sheet music covers of the Michigan fight songs "Varsity" (left) and "The Victors" (right)

The Michigan fight song, "[The Victors](/wiki/The_Victors "The Victors")", was written by student Louis Elbel in 1898. The song was declared by [John Philip Sousa](/wiki/John_Philip_Sousa "John Philip Sousa") to be "the greatest college fight song ever written."[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHondorpAlexis2005118-280) The song refers to the teams as being "the Champions of the West". At the time, the Big Ten Conference was known as the Western Conference.

Although mainly used at sporting events, the Michigan fight song is often heard at other events as well. U.S. President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") had it played by the [United States Marine Band](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Band "United States Marine Band") as his entrance anthem during his term as president from 1974 to 1977, in preference over the more traditional "[Hail to the Chief](/wiki/Hail_to_the_Chief "Hail to the Chief")",[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERozell199238-281) and the Michigan Marching Band performed a slow-tempo variation of the fight song at [his funeral](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Gerald_Ford "Death and state funeral of Gerald Ford"). The fight song is also sung during graduation commencement ceremonies. The university's alma mater song is "[The Yellow and Blue](/wiki/The_Yellow_and_Blue "The Yellow and Blue")". A common rally cry is "[Let's Go Blue](/wiki/Let%27s_Go_Blue "Let's Go Blue")!" which has a complementary short musical arrangement written by former students Joseph Carl and Albert Ahronheim.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Singing_The_Yellow_and_the_Blue_between_halves_of_the_Penn_Game%2C_1916.png/250px-Singing_The_Yellow_and_the_Blue_between_halves_of_the_Penn_Game%2C_1916.png)](/wiki/File:Singing_The_Yellow_and_the_Blue_between_halves_of_the_Penn_Game,_1916.png)

Singing *[The Yellow and the Blue](/wiki/The_Yellow_and_Blue "The Yellow and Blue")* between halves of the [Penn](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") Game, November 1916

Before "The Victors" was officially the university's fight song, the song "[There'll Be a Hot Time in the Old Town Tonight](/wiki/There%27ll_Be_a_Hot_Time_in_the_Old_Town_Tonight "There'll Be a Hot Time in the Old Town Tonight")" was considered to be the school song. After Michigan temporarily withdrew from the Western Conference in 1907, a new Michigan fight song, "[Varsity](/wiki/Varsity_(fight_song) "Varsity (fight song)")", was written in 1911 because the line "champions of the West" was no longer appropriate.

### Accomplishments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Accomplishments")]

The [Michigan football](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") program ranks first in NCAA history in total wins (1,004 through the end of the 2023 season) and tied for 1st among FBS schools in winning percentage (.734). The team won the first [Rose Bowl game](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game") in [1902](/wiki/1902_Rose_Bowl "1902 Rose Bowl"). the university had 40 consecutive winning seasons from 1968 to 2007, including consecutive [bowl game](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game") appearances from 1975 to 2007. The Wolverines have won a record 45 Big Ten championships. The program claims 12 [national championships](/wiki/College_football_national_championships_in_NCAA_Division_I_FBS "College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS"), most recently winning the [2024 National Championship Game](/wiki/2024_College_Football_Playoff_National_Championship "2024 College Football Playoff National Championship"), and has produced three [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") winners: [Tom Harmon](/wiki/Tom_Harmon "Tom Harmon") (1940), [Desmond Howard](/wiki/Desmond_Howard "Desmond Howard") (1991), and [Charles Woodson](/wiki/Charles_Woodson "Charles Woodson") (1997). In 2025, the university made history by becoming the first institution in intercollegiate sports to have first-round draft picks in all five major professional sports leagues (NFL, NBA, NHL, MLB, and MLS) within the same year.[[n 37]](#cite_note-282)

The [men's ice hockey team](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_ice_hockey "Michigan Wolverines men's ice hockey"), which plays at Yost Ice Arena, has won nine [national championships.](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Ice_Hockey_Championship "NCAA Men's Ice Hockey Championship")

The [men's basketball](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_men%27s_basketball "Michigan Wolverines men's basketball") team, which plays at the Crisler Center, has appeared in five [Final Fours](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") and won the [national championship](/wiki/1989_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1989 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") in 1989. The program also voluntarily vacated victories from its 1992–1993 and 1995–1999 seasons in which [illicit payments to players](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_basketball_scandal "University of Michigan basketball scandal") took place, as well as its 1992 and 1993 Final Four appearances. The men's basketball team has most recently won back-to-back Big Ten Tournament Championships.

More than 250 Michigan athletes or coaches have participated in [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") events, and as of 2021 its students and alumni have won [155 Olympic medals](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals"). Through the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), 275 Michigan students and coaches had participated in the Olympics, winning medals in each [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") except 1896, and winning gold medals in all but four Olympiads. the university's students/student-coaches (e.g., notably, Michael Phelps) have won a total of [185 Olympic medals](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines#Olympians "Michigan Wolverines"): 85 golds, 48 silvers, and 52 bronzes.

In 10 of the past 14 years concluding in 2009, the university has finished in the top five of the [NACDA Director's Cup](/wiki/NACDA_Director%27s_Cup "NACDA Director's Cup"), a ranking compiled by the [National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Collegiate_Directors_of_Athletics "National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics") to tabulate the success of universities in competitive sports. The university has finished in the top 10 of the Directors' Cup standings in 21 of the award's 29 seasons between [1993 and 2021](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines#NCAA_Division_I_Directors'_Cup "Michigan Wolverines"), and has placed in the top six in nine of the last 10 seasons.

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Notable people")]

### Benefactors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Benefactors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Henry_Ford_at_1923_UM_vs_Marines_football_game_dedication_of_Yost_Field_House.jpg/250px-Henry_Ford_at_1923_UM_vs_Marines_football_game_dedication_of_Yost_Field_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_Ford_at_1923_UM_vs_Marines_football_game_dedication_of_Yost_Field_House.jpg)

[Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford") (second from the left) at the dedication of [Yost Field House](/wiki/Yost_Field_House "Yost Field House") in 1923

The university received significant assistance in its formation during the 1810s from the [Freemason Zion Lodge of Detroit](/wiki/Zion_Lodge_No._1_F%26AM "Zion Lodge No. 1 F&AM"), which provided much-needed financial support for its establishment. Of the total amount subscribed to start the university, two-thirds came from the Masonic lodge and its members. Notably, the [Campau family](/wiki/Campau_family "Campau family"), led by [Joseph Campau](/wiki/Joseph_Campau "Joseph Campau") (1769–1863) and his nephew [John R. Williams](/wiki/John_R._Williams "John R. Williams") (1782–1854), who was the [first mayor of Detroit](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Detroit "List of mayors of Detroit") under the 1824 charter and whose family members were often referred to as the "Barons of Detroit", made substantial donations toward the construction of the university's first building. Several members of the Campau family were among the earliest graduates of the university, including Alexander Macomb Campau (1823–1908) and his son George Throop Campau (1847–1879).

Other families that contributed to the university include the [Ford](/wiki/Ford_family "Ford family"), [Nichols](/wiki/Nichols_Arboretum "Nichols Arboretum"), [Marsal](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_of_Education "University of Michigan School of Education"), and [Tisch](/wiki/Preston_Robert_Tisch "Preston Robert Tisch") families. Additionally, the [Zell Family Foundation](/wiki/Samuel_Zell "Samuel Zell") and the [Li Ka Shing Foundation](/wiki/Li_Ka_Shing_Foundation "Li Ka Shing Foundation"), based in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), are also listed among the donors. Among the individuals who have made significant donations commemorated at the university are [William Wilson Cook](/wiki/William_Wilson_Cook "William Wilson Cook"), [Dexter Mason Ferry](/wiki/Dexter_M._Ferry "Dexter M. Ferry"), [William Erastus Upjohn](/wiki/William_E._Upjohn "William E. Upjohn"), [John Stoughton Newberry](/wiki/John_Stoughton_Newberry "John Stoughton Newberry"), [Clara Harrison Stranahan](/wiki/Clara_Harrison_Stranahan "Clara Harrison Stranahan"), [William K. Brehm](/wiki/William_K._Brehm "William K. Brehm"), [William Morse Davidson](/wiki/William_Morse_Davidson "William Morse Davidson"), [A. Alfred Taubman](/wiki/A._Alfred_Taubman "A. Alfred Taubman"), [Penny W. Stamps](/wiki/Penny_W._Stamps_School_of_Art_and_Design "Penny W. Stamps School of Art and Design"), [Stephen M. Ross](/wiki/Stephen_M._Ross "Stephen M. Ross"), [Charles Munger](/wiki/Charles_Munger "Charles Munger"), and [Ronald Weiser](/wiki/Ronald_Weiser "Ronald Weiser").

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Faculty")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan faculty and staff](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_faculty_and_staff "List of University of Michigan faculty and staff").

The university employs 8,189 faculty members,[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202471–82-283) of whom 3,195 are tenured or on a tenure track.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas202471–82-283) Among them, there are 37 members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences"),[[w 48]](#cite_note-284) 62 members of the [National Academy of Medicine](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Medicine "National Academy of Medicine"),[[w 49]](#cite_note-285) 30 members of the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering"),[[w 50]](#cite_note-286) 89 [Sloan Research Fellows](/wiki/Sloan_Research_Fellowship "Sloan Research Fellowship"),[[w 51]](#cite_note-287) 17 [Guggenheim Fellows](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellows "Guggenheim Fellows"),[[w 52]](#cite_note-288) 99 members of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences"),[[w 53]](#cite_note-289) and 17 members of the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society").[[w 54]](#cite_note-290)[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELombardiCapaldiReevesGater2004-291) The university's current and former faculty includes fourteen [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_faculty_and_staff#Nobel_Laureates "List of University of Michigan faculty and staff"), eight [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners, three David M. Holland Medal winners, and one [John Bates Clark Medal](/wiki/John_Bates_Clark_Medal "John Bates Clark Medal") recipient.

Current faculty include physicists [Mark Newman](/wiki/Mark_Newman "Mark Newman"), [Duncan G. Steel](/wiki/Duncan_G._Steel "Duncan G. Steel"), [Steven Cundiff](/wiki/Steven_Cundiff "Steven Cundiff"), [Stephen Forrest](/wiki/Stephen_Forrest "Stephen Forrest"), and [Gordon Kane](/wiki/Gordon_Kane "Gordon Kane"); mathematicians [Hyman Bass](/wiki/Hyman_Bass "Hyman Bass"), [Sergey Fomin](/wiki/Sergey_Fomin "Sergey Fomin"), [William Fulton](/wiki/William_Fulton_(mathematician) "William Fulton (mathematician)"), [Robert Griess](/wiki/Robert_Griess "Robert Griess"), and [Melvin Hochster](/wiki/Melvin_Hochster "Melvin Hochster"); chemist [Melanie Sanford](/wiki/Melanie_Sanford "Melanie Sanford"); Pulitzer Prize-winning historian [Heather Ann Thompson](/wiki/Heather_Ann_Thompson "Heather Ann Thompson"); [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") recipients [Huda Akil](/wiki/Huda_Akil "Huda Akil") and [Robert Axelrod](/wiki/Robert_Axelrod_(political_scientist) "Robert Axelrod (political scientist)"); biostatistician [Gonçalo Abecasis](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Abecasis "Gonçalo Abecasis"); philosophers [Elizabeth S. Anderson](/wiki/Elizabeth_S._Anderson "Elizabeth S. Anderson"), [Allan Gibbard](/wiki/Allan_Gibbard "Allan Gibbard"), and [Peter Railton](/wiki/Peter_Railton "Peter Railton"); and social psychologist [Richard E. Nisbett](/wiki/Richard_E._Nisbett "Richard E. Nisbett"). The faculty also includes feminist legal theorist [Catharine MacKinnon](/wiki/Catharine_MacKinnon "Catharine MacKinnon"), *[Strict Scrutiny](/wiki/Strict_Scrutiny "Strict Scrutiny")* co-host [Leah Litman](/wiki/Leah_Litman "Leah Litman"), engineer [James P. Bagian](/wiki/James_P._Bagian "James P. Bagian"), and [A. Galip Ulsoy](/wiki/A._Galip_Ulsoy "A. Galip Ulsoy"), co-inventor of the [reconfigurable manufacturing system](/wiki/Reconfigurable_manufacturing_system "Reconfigurable manufacturing system").

Notable faculty in physics have included [Donald A. Glaser](/wiki/Donald_A._Glaser "Donald A. Glaser"), the inventor of the [bubble chamber](/wiki/Bubble_chamber "Bubble chamber"); [Samuel Goudsmit](/wiki/Samuel_Goudsmit "Samuel Goudsmit") and [George Uhlenbeck](/wiki/George_Uhlenbeck "George Uhlenbeck"), discoverers of [electron spin](/wiki/Electron_spin "Electron spin"); [Kazimierz Fajans](/wiki/Kazimierz_Fajans "Kazimierz Fajans"), co-discoverer of [protactinium](/wiki/Protactinium "Protactinium"); [Peter Franken](/wiki/Peter_Franken "Peter Franken"), who first demonstrated the [second-harmonic generation](/wiki/Second-harmonic_generation "Second-harmonic generation"); [Juris Upatnieks](/wiki/Juris_Upatnieks "Juris Upatnieks") and [Emmett Leith](/wiki/Emmett_Leith "Emmett Leith"), inventors of [3D holography](/wiki/3D_holography "3D holography"); [Gérard Mourou](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Mourou "Gérard Mourou"), inventor of [chirped pulse amplification](/wiki/Chirped_pulse_amplification "Chirped pulse amplification"); and [H. Richard Crane](/wiki/H._Richard_Crane "H. Richard Crane"), who was called "one of the most distinguished experimental physicists of the 20th century". [Wolfgang Pauli](/wiki/Wolfgang_Pauli "Wolfgang Pauli"), a pioneer of [quantum physics](/wiki/Quantum_physics "Quantum physics"), served as a visiting professor at the university in 1931 and again in 1941. Physicists [Martin Lewis Perl](/wiki/Martin_Lewis_Perl "Martin Lewis Perl") and [Lawrence W. Jones](/wiki/Lawrence_W._Jones "Lawrence W. Jones") served as co-advisors to Nobel laureate [Samuel C. C. Ting](/wiki/Samuel_C._C._Ting "Samuel C. C. Ting") at the university. Perl himself was also awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1995 for his discovery of the [tau lepton](/wiki/Tau_lepton "Tau lepton"). Other distinguished physicists who have served on the faculty include [Martinus Veltman](/wiki/Martinus_Veltman "Martinus Veltman"), [Carl Wieman](/wiki/Carl_Wieman "Carl Wieman"), and [Ernest Courant](/wiki/Ernest_Courant "Ernest Courant"). Notable mathematicians [Raoul Bott](/wiki/Raoul_Bott "Raoul Bott"), [Richard Brauer](/wiki/Richard_Brauer "Richard Brauer"), [Samuel Eilenberg](/wiki/Samuel_Eilenberg "Samuel Eilenberg") (co-founder of [category theory](/wiki/Category_theory "Category theory")), [Frederick Gehring](/wiki/Frederick_Gehring "Frederick Gehring"), [Herman Goldstine](/wiki/Herman_Goldstine "Herman Goldstine"), and [Anatol Rapoport](/wiki/Anatol_Rapoport "Anatol Rapoport") have all served on the faculty.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Josef_Brodsky.jpg/250px-Josef_Brodsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Josef_Brodsky.jpg)

Photograph of [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky") teaching at the University of Michigan

Philosophers who were members of the faculty include [pragmatists](/wiki/Pragmatists "Pragmatists") [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey"), [Charles Horton Cooley](/wiki/Charles_Horton_Cooley "Charles Horton Cooley"), and [George H. Mead](/wiki/George_H._Mead "George H. Mead"), along with [analytic philosophers](/wiki/Analytic_philosophers "Analytic philosophers") [William Frankena](/wiki/William_Frankena "William Frankena") and [Cooper Harold Langford](/wiki/Cooper_Harold_Langford "Cooper Harold Langford"). The faculty included notable writers such as Nobel Prize-winning essayist [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky"), Pulitzer Prize-winning poet [W. H. Auden](/wiki/W._H._Auden "W. H. Auden"), and [Robert Frost](/wiki/Robert_Frost "Robert Frost"), the only poet to receive four Pulitzer Prizes for Poetry.

In medicine, notable individuals who have served on the faculty include the former director of the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health") [Francis Collins](/wiki/Francis_Collins "Francis Collins"), the developer of the [polio vaccine](/wiki/Polio_vaccine "Polio vaccine") [Jonas Salk](/wiki/Jonas_Salk "Jonas Salk"), Nobel Prize–winning physiologist [Charles B. Huggins](/wiki/Charles_B._Huggins "Charles B. Huggins"), co-discoverer of [tumour-inducing viruses](/wiki/Oncovirus "Oncovirus") [Peyton Rous](/wiki/Peyton_Rous "Peyton Rous"), geneticist [James V. Neel](/wiki/James_V._Neel "James V. Neel"), neuroanatomist [Elizabeth C. Crosby](/wiki/Elizabeth_C._Crosby "Elizabeth C. Crosby"), and co-discoverer of [restriction enzymes](/wiki/Restriction_enzymes "Restriction enzymes") [Hamilton O. Smith](/wiki/Hamilton_O._Smith "Hamilton O. Smith").

Past faculty have also included "the founding father of 21st-century sociology" [Charles Tilly](/wiki/Charles_Tilly "Charles Tilly"), social psychologist [Robert Zajonc](/wiki/Robert_Zajonc "Robert Zajonc"), chemical engineer [Donald L. Katz](/wiki/Donald_L._Katz "Donald L. Katz"), Supreme Court justice [Henry Billings Brown](/wiki/Henry_Billings_Brown "Henry Billings Brown"), Pulitzer Prize-winning composer [Leslie Bassett](/wiki/Leslie_Bassett "Leslie Bassett"), Pulitzer Prize-winning photographer [David C. Turnley](/wiki/David_C._Turnley "David C. Turnley"), Nobel Prize-winning economist [Lawrence R. Klein](/wiki/Lawrence_R._Klein "Lawrence R. Klein"), and [John Bates Clark Medal](/wiki/John_Bates_Clark_Medal "John Bates Clark Medal") recipient [Kenneth E. Boulding](/wiki/Kenneth_E._Boulding "Kenneth E. Boulding").

### Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Alumni")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may be [too long](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_size "Wikipedia:Article size") to read and navigate comfortably**. Consider [splitting](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting") content into sub-articles, [condensing](/wiki/Wikipedia:Summary_style "Wikipedia:Summary style") it, or adding [subheadings](/wiki/Help:Section#Subsections "Help:Section"). Please discuss this issue on the article's [talk page](/wiki/Talk:University_of_Michigan "Talk:University of Michigan"). *(March 2025)* |

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni "List of University of Michigan alumni").

Michigan alumni include nine [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Nobel_laureates "List of University of Michigan alumni"), two [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize") winners ([Isadore M. Singer](/wiki/Isadore_M._Singer "Isadore M. Singer") and [Karen Uhlenbeck](/wiki/Karen_Uhlenbeck "Karen Uhlenbeck")), two [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medalists "Fields Medalists") ([June Huh](/wiki/June_Huh "June Huh") and [Stephen Smale](/wiki/Stephen_Smale "Stephen Smale")), five [Turing Award winners](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Turing_Award_winners "List of University of Michigan alumni"), and 35 [Pulitzer Prize winners](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Pulitzer_Prize_winners "List of University of Michigan alumni"). By alumni count, Michigan ranks fifth as of 2018,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit) among all universities whose alumni have won Pulitzers.

#### Mathematics and sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Mathematics and sciences")]

See also: [List of University of Michigan alumni § Mathematics](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Mathematics "List of University of Michigan alumni"), and [List of University of Michigan alumni § Science](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Science "List of University of Michigan alumni")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Jerome_Karle_Portrait.jpg/250px-Jerome_Karle_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerome_Karle_Portrait.jpg)

[Jerome Karle](/wiki/Jerome_Karle "Jerome Karle")

[Claude Shannon](/wiki/Claude_Shannon "Claude Shannon"), who laid the foundations of the [Information Age](/wiki/Information_Age "Information Age"), ranks among the most distinguished mathematicians from the university. Two [Fields medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") [Stephen Smale](/wiki/Stephen_Smale "Stephen Smale") and [June Huh](/wiki/June_Huh "June Huh"), both completed their Ph.D.s in Mathematics at Michigan. [Isadore Singer](/wiki/Isadore_Singer "Isadore Singer"), the [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize")-winning mathematician who helped prove the [Atiyah–Singer index theorem](/wiki/Atiyah%E2%80%93Singer_index_theorem "Atiyah–Singer index theorem"), studied physics at the university during World War II. [Karen Uhlenbeck](/wiki/Karen_Uhlenbeck "Karen Uhlenbeck"), the first woman to win the Abel Prize, received her bachelor's degree from the university in 1964. [George Dantzig](/wiki/George_Dantzig "George Dantzig"), who developed [linear programming](/wiki/Linear_programming "Linear programming"), studied at Michigan under [G.Y. Rainich](/wiki/George_Yuri_Rainich "George Yuri Rainich"), [R.L. Wilder](/wiki/R.L._Wilder "R.L. Wilder"), and [T.H. Hildebrandt](/wiki/Theophil_Henry_Hildebrandt "Theophil Henry Hildebrandt"). Other mathematicians from the university include [Kenneth Ira Appel](/wiki/Kenneth_Ira_Appel "Kenneth Ira Appel"), who, along with [Wolfgang Haken](/wiki/Wolfgang_Haken "Wolfgang Haken"), solved the famous [Four Color Theorem](/wiki/Four_Color_Theorem "Four Color Theorem"); [Leonard Jimmie Savage](/wiki/Leonard_Jimmie_Savage "Leonard Jimmie Savage"), who was known for his contributions to [Bayesian statistics](/wiki/Bayesian_statistics "Bayesian statistics") and [decision theory](/wiki/Decision_theory "Decision theory"); and [Carl R. de Boor](/wiki/Carl_R._de_Boor "Carl R. de Boor"), a renowned mathematician in [numerical analysis](/wiki/Numerical_analysis "Numerical analysis").

In physics, Nobel laureate [Samuel C. C. Ting](/wiki/Samuel_C._C._Ting "Samuel C. C. Ting"), who discovered the [J/ψ particle](/wiki/J/%CF%88_particle "J/ψ particle"), studied under [Martin Lewis Perl](/wiki/Martin_Lewis_Perl "Martin Lewis Perl"), another Nobel-winning physicist who discovered the [tau lepton](/wiki/Tau_lepton "Tau lepton"), at the university. Theoretical physicist [Elmer Imes](/wiki/Elmer_Imes "Elmer Imes"), who demonstrated the application of quantum theory to the rotational energy of molecules, was the second African American to earn a Ph.D. in physics. Chemist [Jerome Karle](/wiki/Jerome_Karle "Jerome Karle"), who revealed molecular structures, completed his Ph.D. in Physics at Michigan in 1943. His wife, [Isabella Karle](/wiki/Isabella_Karle "Isabella Karle"), an alumna, developed techniques to extract [plutonium chloride](/wiki/Plutonium_chloride "Plutonium chloride") from [plutonium oxide](/wiki/Plutonium_oxide "Plutonium oxide") mixtures. Other alumni include nuclear physicist [Robert Bacher](/wiki/Robert_Bacher "Robert Bacher"), a leader of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"); [Richard Smalley](/wiki/Richard_Smalley "Richard Smalley"), who discovered [fullerenes](/wiki/Fullerenes "Fullerenes"); [Moses Gomberg](/wiki/Moses_Gomberg "Moses Gomberg"), a pioneer in [radical chemistry](/wiki/Radical_chemistry "Radical chemistry"), who later became a professor at the university and taught [Frank Spedding](/wiki/Frank_Spedding "Frank Spedding"), who led the development of the [Ames process](/wiki/Ames_process "Ames process") in the Manhattan Project.

Alumni in biology and medicine include [Marshall Warren Nirenberg](/wiki/Marshall_Warren_Nirenberg "Marshall Warren Nirenberg"), famous for breaking the [genetic code](/wiki/Genetic_code "Genetic code"), who received his Ph.D. in biochemistry from the university in 1957; [Stanley Cohen](/wiki/Stanley_Cohen_(biochemist) "Stanley Cohen (biochemist)"), who discovered [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factors "Growth factors"); and [John Jacob Abel](/wiki/John_Jacob_Abel "John Jacob Abel"), regarded as the father of [pharmacology](/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology"), who studied under the university's physiologist Henry Sewall in 1883. Other alumni include cardiologist [Frank Norman Wilson](/wiki/Frank_Norman_Wilson "Frank Norman Wilson"); bacteriologist [Frederick George Novy](/wiki/Frederick_George_Novy "Frederick George Novy"); biologist [Raymond Pearl](/wiki/Raymond_Pearl "Raymond Pearl"); and [David Botstein](/wiki/David_Botstein "David Botstein"), a leader of the [Human Genome Project](/wiki/Human_Genome_Project "Human Genome Project").

Other notable alumni in science include [Edgar F. Codd](/wiki/Edgar_F._Codd "Edgar F. Codd"), who developed the [relational model of data](/wiki/Relational_model "Relational model") and completed his Ph.D. at Michigan, and computer scientist [Michael Stonebraker](/wiki/Michael_Stonebraker "Michael Stonebraker"), who also made contributions to [database](/wiki/Database_systems "Database systems") research. Both Codd and Stonebraker are [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winners.

#### Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Law and government")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan law and government alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_law_and_government_alumni "List of University of Michigan law and government alumni").

See also: [List of University of Michigan Law School alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_Law_School_alumni "List of University of Michigan Law School alumni")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Gerald_Ford_at_University_of_Michigan_1976.png/250px-Gerald_Ford_at_University_of_Michigan_1976.png)](/wiki/File:Gerald_Ford_at_University_of_Michigan_1976.png)

U.S. President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") wearing a "Michigan #1" sweater during the kick-off of Ford's [1976 presidential campaign](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election") at the University of Michigan's campus in [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor "Ann Arbor")

The university boasts several holders or candidates of the [United States presidency](/wiki/United_States_presidency "United States presidency"), including [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), the 38th president and the Republican Party's nominee for president in [1976](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election"); [Thomas E. Dewey](/wiki/Thomas_E._Dewey "Thomas E. Dewey"), who was the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")'s nominee for president in both [1944](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election") and [1948](/wiki/1948_United_States_presidential_election "1948 United States presidential election"); [Arthur LeSueur](/wiki/Arthur_LeSueur "Arthur LeSueur"), a [Socialist](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") candidate for president in [1916](/wiki/1916_United_States_presidential_election "1916 United States presidential election"); [Gilbert Hitchcock](/wiki/Gilbert_Hitchcock "Gilbert Hitchcock"), a [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") candidate in [1928](/wiki/1928_United_States_presidential_election "1928 United States presidential election"); [Arthur Vandenberg](/wiki/Arthur_Vandenberg "Arthur Vandenberg"), a Republican presidential hopeful in 1948; [Ben Carson](/wiki/Ben_Carson "Ben Carson"), a Republican candidate in [2016](/wiki/Ben_Carson_2016_presidential_campaign "Ben Carson 2016 presidential campaign"); and [Larry Elder](/wiki/Larry_Elder "Larry Elder"), a Republican candidate in [2024](/wiki/Larry_Elder_2024_presidential_campaign "Larry Elder 2024 presidential campaign"). [John Worth Kern](/wiki/John_Worth_Kern "John Worth Kern") and [Burton K. Wheeler](/wiki/Burton_K._Wheeler "Burton K. Wheeler") both ran for the [vice presidency](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States"), with Kern representing the Democratic Party alongside [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") in [1908](/wiki/1908_United_States_presidential_election "1908 United States presidential election"), and Wheeler as a [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1924%E2%80%931934) "Progressive Party (United States, 1924–1934)") nominee with [Robert La Follette Sr.](/wiki/Robert_La_Follette_Sr. "Robert La Follette Sr.") in [1924](/wiki/1924_United_States_presidential_election "1924 United States presidential election").

Among the 23 former [governors of Michigan](/wiki/Governors_of_Michigan "Governors of Michigan") who hold formal college degrees, 10 are graduates of the university. ([Woodbridge N. Ferris](/wiki/Woodbridge_N._Ferris "Woodbridge N. Ferris") only attended for a year) As of 2021, the university has matriculated 63 [U.S. governors](/wiki/U.S._governors "U.S. governors") or lieutenant governors, including former Governor of Michigan [Rick Snyder](/wiki/Rick_Snyder "Rick Snyder"), first female [lieutenant governor of Missouri](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_of_Missouri "Lieutenant governor of Missouri") [Harriett Woods](/wiki/Harriett_Woods "Harriett Woods"), and former [Governor of California](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") [Culbert Olson](/wiki/Culbert_Olson "Culbert Olson"). More than 250 Michigan graduates have served as [legislators](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_legislator_alumni "List of University of Michigan legislator alumni") as either a [United States Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator") (48 graduates) or as a [Congressional representative](/wiki/United_States_Representatives "United States Representatives") (over 215 graduates), including former [House Majority Leader](/wiki/House_Majority_Leader "House Majority Leader") [Dick Gephardt](/wiki/Dick_Gephardt "Dick Gephardt"), U.S. Representative [Justin Amash](/wiki/Justin_Amash "Justin Amash"). Former [Los Angeles Mayor](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Mayor "Los Angeles Mayor") [Richard Riordan](/wiki/Richard_Riordan "Richard Riordan"), former [Chicago Mayor](/wiki/Chicago_Mayor "Chicago Mayor") [Lori Lightfoot](/wiki/Lori_Lightfoot "Lori Lightfoot"), and [Detroit Mayor](/wiki/Detroit_Mayor "Detroit Mayor") [Mike Duggan](/wiki/Mike_Duggan "Mike Duggan") are also Michigan graduates.

Michigan graduates have held a range of cabinet-level positions, including [United States Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") ([William Rufus Day](/wiki/William_Rufus_Day "William Rufus Day")); [United States Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") ([George M. Humphrey](/wiki/George_M._Humphrey "George M. Humphrey")); [United States Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") ([Harry Micajah Daugherty](/wiki/Harry_Micajah_Daugherty "Harry Micajah Daugherty")); [United States Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") ([Kenneth Lee Salazar](/wiki/Kenneth_Lee_Salazar "Kenneth Lee Salazar")); [United States Secretary of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Agriculture "United States Secretary of Agriculture") ([Clinton Anderson](/wiki/Clinton_Anderson_(New_Mexico_politician) "Clinton Anderson (New Mexico politician)"), [Julius Sterling Morton](/wiki/Julius_Sterling_Morton "Julius Sterling Morton"), [Arthur M. Hyde](/wiki/Arthur_M._Hyde "Arthur M. Hyde"), and [Dan Glickman](/wiki/Dan_Glickman "Dan Glickman")); [United States Secretary of Commerce](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Commerce "United States Secretary of Commerce") ([Roy D. Chapin](/wiki/Roy_D._Chapin "Roy D. Chapin") and [Robert P. Lamont](/wiki/Robert_P._Lamont "Robert P. Lamont")); [United States Secretary of Health and Human Services](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Secretary of Health and Human Services") ([Tom Price](/wiki/Tom_Price_(American_politician) "Tom Price (American politician)")); and Director of the [United States Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Management_and_Budget "United States Office of Management and Budget") ([Rob Portman](/wiki/Rob_Portman "Rob Portman")). Multiple alumni served in the judicial branch of the U.S. government, including William Rufus Day, [Frank Murphy](/wiki/Frank_Murphy "Frank Murphy"), and [George Sutherland](/wiki/George_Sutherland "George Sutherland"), all of whom served as [Supreme Court justices](/wiki/Associate_Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States"). As of 2019, the university had placed onto various state supreme courts over 125 graduates, 40 of whom served as chief justice.

Foreign alumni include the [Prime Minister of Singapore](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Singapore "Prime Minister of Singapore") ([Lawrence Wong](/wiki/Lawrence_Wong "Lawrence Wong")); the current ruler of the [Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah](/wiki/Emirate_of_Ras_Al_Khaimah "Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah") ([Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi](/wiki/Saud_bin_Saqr_Al_Qasimi "Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi")); the 51st [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") ([Lamberto Dini](/wiki/Lamberto_Dini "Lamberto Dini")); the [Prime Minister of Antigua and Barbuda](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Antigua_and_Barbuda "Prime Minister of Antigua and Barbuda") 1994–2004 ([Lester Bird](/wiki/Lester_Bird "Lester Bird")); the 47th [President of Costa Rica](/wiki/President_of_Costa_Rica "President of Costa Rica") ([Luis Guillermo Solís](/wiki/Luis_Guillermo_Sol%C3%ADs "Luis Guillermo Solís")); the [Prime Minister of Peru](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Peru "Prime Minister of Peru") 1993–1994 ([Alfonso Bustamante](/wiki/Alfonso_Bustamante "Alfonso Bustamante")); the [Prime Minister of Jordan](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Jordan "Prime Minister of Jordan") 2012–2016 ([Abdullah Ensour](/wiki/Abdullah_Ensour "Abdullah Ensour")); the 13th [President of Pakistan](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan") ([Arif Alvi](/wiki/Arif_Alvi "Arif Alvi")); [Chief Secretary of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chief_Secretary_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Secretary of Hong Kong") 2007–2011 ([Henry Tang Ying-yen](/wiki/Henry_Tang_Ying-yen "Henry Tang Ying-yen")); [Deputy Prime Minister of South Korea](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_South_Korea "Deputy Prime Minister of South Korea") 2017–2018 ([Kim Dong-yeon](/wiki/Kim_Dong-yeon "Kim Dong-yeon")); [Deputy Prime Minister of Bulgaria](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Bulgaria "Deputy Prime Minister of Bulgaria") in the government of Boyko Borisov ([Simeon Djankov](/wiki/Simeon_Djankov "Simeon Djankov")); Deputy Prime Minister of [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") 1997–1998 ([Herizo Razafimahaleo](/wiki/Herizo_Razafimahaleo "Herizo Razafimahaleo")). British [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") [Terry Davis](/wiki/Terry_Davis_(politician) "Terry Davis (politician)") and [Howard Flight](/wiki/Howard_Flight "Howard Flight") are also Michigan graduates. As of 2022, Michigan has matriculated 64 ambassadors who have served in more than 72 countries.

#### Business and finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Business and finance")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan business alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_business_alumni "List of University of Michigan business alumni").

([Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page")), [Johnson & Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_%26_Johnson "Johnson & Johnson") ([Edward Mead Johnson](/wiki/Edward_Mead_Johnson "Edward Mead Johnson")), [Abbott Laboratories](/wiki/Abbott_Laboratories "Abbott Laboratories") (Wallace Calvin Abbott), [Stryker Corporation](/wiki/Stryker_Corporation "Stryker Corporation") ([Homer Stryker](/wiki/Homer_Stryker "Homer Stryker")), [Emerson Electric Company](/wiki/Emerson_Electric_Company "Emerson Electric Company") ([John Wesley Emerson](/wiki/John_Wesley_Emerson "John Wesley Emerson")), [Loews Corporation](/wiki/Loews_Corporation "Loews Corporation") ([Preston Robert Tisch](/wiki/Preston_Robert_Tisch "Preston Robert Tisch")), [Mayo Clinic](/wiki/Mayo_Clinic "Mayo Clinic") ([William James Mayo](/wiki/William_James_Mayo "William James Mayo")), [Devon Energy](/wiki/Devon_Energy "Devon Energy") ([J. #### Literature, music and theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Literature, music and theatre")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan arts alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_arts_alumni "List of University of Michigan arts alumni").

Notable writers who attended the university include playwright [Arthur Miller](/wiki/Arthur_Miller "Arthur Miller"), essayists [Susan Orlean](/wiki/Susan_Orlean "Susan Orlean"), [Jia Tolentino](/wiki/Jia_Tolentino "Jia Tolentino"), [Sven Birkerts](/wiki/Sven_Birkerts "Sven Birkerts"), journalists and editors [Mike Wallace](/wiki/Mike_Wallace "Mike Wallace"), [Jonathan Chait](/wiki/Jonathan_Chait "Jonathan Chait") of *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")*, Indian author and columnist [Anees Jung](/wiki/Anees_Jung "Anees Jung"), [Daniel Okrent](/wiki/Daniel_Okrent "Daniel Okrent"), and [Sandra Steingraber](/wiki/Sandra_Steingraber "Sandra Steingraber"), food critics [Ruth Reichl](/wiki/Ruth_Reichl "Ruth Reichl") and [Gael Greene](/wiki/Gael_Greene "Gael Greene"), novelists [Brett Ellen Block](/wiki/Brett_Ellen_Block "Brett Ellen Block"), [Elizabeth Kostova](/wiki/Elizabeth_Kostova "Elizabeth Kostova"), [Marge Piercy](/wiki/Marge_Piercy "Marge Piercy"), [Brad Meltzer](/wiki/Brad_Meltzer "Brad Meltzer"), [Betty Smith](/wiki/Betty_Smith "Betty Smith"), and [Charles Major](/wiki/Charles_Major_(writer) "Charles Major (writer)"), screenwriter [Judith Guest](/wiki/Judith_Guest "Judith Guest"), Pulitzer Prize-winning poet [Theodore Roethke](/wiki/Theodore_Roethke "Theodore Roethke"), National Book Award winners [Keith Waldrop](/wiki/Keith_Waldrop "Keith Waldrop") and [Jesmyn Ward](/wiki/Jesmyn_Ward "Jesmyn Ward"), composer/author/puppeteer [Forman Brown](/wiki/Forman_Brown "Forman Brown"), [Alireza Jafarzadeh](/wiki/Alireza_Jafarzadeh "Alireza Jafarzadeh") (a Middle East analyst, author, and TV commentator), and memoirist and self-help book author [Jerry Newport](/wiki/Jerry_and_Mary_Newport "Jerry and Mary Newport").

Musical graduates include operatic soprano [Jessye Norman](/wiki/Jessye_Norman "Jessye Norman"), singer [Joe Dassin](/wiki/Joe_Dassin "Joe Dassin"), multiple members of the bands [Tally Hall](/wiki/Tally_Hall "Tally Hall") and [Vulfpeck](/wiki/Vulfpeck "Vulfpeck"), jazz guitarist [Randy Napoleon](/wiki/Randy_Napoleon "Randy Napoleon"), and [Mannheim Steamroller](/wiki/Mannheim_Steamroller "Mannheim Steamroller") founder [Chip Davis](/wiki/Chip_Davis "Chip Davis"). Well-known composers who are alumni include [Frank Ticheli](/wiki/Frank_Ticheli "Frank Ticheli"), [Andrew Lippa](/wiki/Andrew_Lippa "Andrew Lippa"), and the Oscar and Tony Award-winning duo [Benj Pasek and Justin Paul](/wiki/Pasek_and_Paul "Pasek and Paul"). Pop superstar [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(entertainer) "Madonna (entertainer)") and rock legend [Iggy Pop](/wiki/Iggy_Pop "Iggy Pop") attended but did not graduate.

In [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States"), famous alumni include actors [Michael Dunn](/wiki/Michael_Dunn_(actor) "Michael Dunn (actor)"), [Darren Criss](/wiki/Darren_Criss "Darren Criss"), [James Earl Jones](/wiki/James_Earl_Jones "James Earl Jones"), and [David Alan Grier](/wiki/David_Alan_Grier "David Alan Grier"); actresses [Lucy Liu](/wiki/Lucy_Liu "Lucy Liu"), [Gilda Radner](/wiki/Gilda_Radner "Gilda Radner"), and [Selma Blair](/wiki/Selma_Blair "Selma Blair") as well as television director [Mark Cendrowski](/wiki/Mark_Cendrowski "Mark Cendrowski") and filmmaker [Lawrence Kasdan](/wiki/Lawrence_Kasdan "Lawrence Kasdan"). Many Broadway and musical theatre actors, including [Gavin Creel](/wiki/Gavin_Creel "Gavin Creel"), [Andrew Keenan-Bolger](/wiki/Andrew_Keenan-Bolger "Andrew Keenan-Bolger"), his sister [Celia Keenan-Bolger](/wiki/Celia_Keenan-Bolger "Celia Keenan-Bolger"), [Taylor Louderman](/wiki/Taylor_Louderman "Taylor Louderman"), and [Blake Roman](/wiki/Blake_Roman "Blake Roman") attended the university for musical theatre. Emmy Award winner [Sanjay Gupta](/wiki/Sanjay_Gupta "Sanjay Gupta") attended both college and medical school at the university.

#### Engineering and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Engineering and technology")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan alumni § Computers, engineering, and technology](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_alumni#Computers,_engineering,_and_technology "List of University of Michigan alumni").

Many alumni have made significant contributions to the fields of engineering and technology, including [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin") engineer [Clarence "Kelly" Johnson](/wiki/Clarence_%22Kelly%22_Johnson "Clarence \"Kelly\" Johnson") and [Joseph Francis Shea](/wiki/Joseph_Francis_Shea "Joseph Francis Shea"), a key figure in the [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program"), earned his Ph.D. in 1955. The university produced numerous developers and original authors of widely recognized software programs, such as [Thomas Knoll](/wiki/Thomas_Knoll "Thomas Knoll") (original author of [Adobe Photoshop](/wiki/Adobe_Photoshop "Adobe Photoshop")); [Mike Engelhardt](/wiki/Mike_Engelhardt "Mike Engelhardt") (original author of [LTspice](/wiki/LTspice "LTspice")); [Niels Provos](/wiki/Niels_Provos "Niels Provos") (creator of [Bcrypt](/wiki/Bcrypt "Bcrypt")); and [Sid Meier](/wiki/Sid_Meier "Sid Meier") (creator of video game series *[Civilization](/wiki/Civilization_(series) "Civilization (series)")*).

Several astronauts attended Michigan including the all-Michigan crews of both [Gemini 4](/wiki/Gemini_4 "Gemini 4"),[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShayler2001103-293) and [Apollo 15](/wiki/Apollo_15 "Apollo 15"). The university claims the only alumni association with a chapter on the Moon, established in 1971 when the crew of [Apollo 15](/wiki/Apollo_15 "Apollo 15") placed a charter plaque for a new University of Michigan Alumni Association on the lunar surface.

#### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Sports")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Michigan sporting alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Michigan_sporting_alumni "List of University of Michigan sporting alumni").

Famous athletes who attended the university include professional football quarterback [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady"), and Olympic swimmer [Michael Phelps](/wiki/Michael_Phelps "Michael Phelps"). [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") players [Zach Hyman](/wiki/Zach_Hyman "Zach Hyman"), [Brendan Morrison](/wiki/Brendan_Morrison "Brendan Morrison"), and [Michael Cammalleri](/wiki/Michael_Cammalleri "Michael Cammalleri") all played for the university's ice hockey team. [National Baseball Hall of Famers](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "List of members of the National Baseball Hall of Fame") [George Sisler](/wiki/George_Sisler "George Sisler") and [Barry Larkin](/wiki/Barry_Larkin "Barry Larkin") also played baseball at the university.

#### Other notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Michigan&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Other notable alumni")]

Activists associated with the university include [Weather Underground](/wiki/Weather_Underground "Weather Underground") leader [Bill Ayers](/wiki/Bill_Ayers "Bill Ayers"),[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlsson2014112-294) activist [Tom Hayden](/wiki/Tom_Hayden "Tom Hayden"), assisted-suicide advocate [Jack Kevorkian](/wiki/Jack_Kevorkian "Jack Kevorkian"). Notable criminals include the Unabomber [Ted Kaczynski](/wiki/Ted_Kaczynski "Ted Kaczynski"), America’s first serial Killer [H.H. Holmes](/wiki/H.H._Holmes "H.H. Holmes"), mass murderer [John Emil List](/wiki/John_Emil_List "John Emil List"), convicted sex offender [Larry Nassar](/wiki/Larry_Nassar "Larry Nassar"), and kidnappers and murderers of 14-year old Bobby Franks, [Nathan F. Leopold, Jr.](/wiki/Leopold_and_Loeb "Leopold and Loeb") and [Richard Albert Loeb](/wiki/Richard_Albert_Loeb "Richard Albert Loeb").

1. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** The *séminaires* in the colonies were primarily institutional transplants from France. In terms of their spirit and mission to prepare individuals for the priesthood, these institutions closely resembled their counterparts in France, which were often advanced ecclesiastical establishments. The training involved rigorous academic formation, including the defense of theses. However, the realities of a frontier society meant that they could not simply exist as carbon copies of those in France. In their early years, in an attempt by Jean-Baptiste Colbert, King Louis XIV's minister, to impose the French language and culture on the colonies, the *séminaires* in New France mainly served as preparatory schools for Indigenous people as well as for children of settlers with studious dispositions and a desire to enter the priesthood. They would only later evolve into true *séminaires* when circumstances allowed.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagnuson19923–15-13)
